1997.assessment: Fiscal health data unavailable for 1997. Taliban controlled most of Afghanistan but ran no formal budget or Treasury. International sanctions prohibited financial flows. IMF had no engagement. Opium economy dominant.

2001.assessment: Fiscal health data unavailable for 2001. Taliban controlled most of Afghanistan but ran no formal budget or Treasury. International sanctions prohibited financial flows. IMF had no engagement. Opium economy dominant.

2002.assessment:
Afghanistan's fiscal balance was essentially zero at -0.10% of GDP in 2002, the first year
of the post-Taliban transitional government. Public debt was extreme at approximately 346%
of GDP, largely consisting of arrears to Soviet-era creditors and other legacy obligations.
The government was almost entirely dependent on international aid to function.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01
IMF, Total Government Debt for Afghanistan (AFGGGDGDPGDPPT) via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
A modest fiscal deficit of -2.1% of GDP in 2003, while public debt remained astronomically
high at ~271% of GDP. The transitional government had very limited domestic revenue and
relied almost entirely on donor grants. Debt relief negotiations with Paris Club creditors
were underway to address the unsustainable debt overhang.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
Fiscal deficit remained at -2.4% of GDP in 2004, with public debt declining to ~245% of
GDP as the economy grew. The government made progress building domestic revenue collection.
International debt relief resulted in significant debt write-offs during this period.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
The fiscal deficit narrowed significantly to -1.0% of GDP in 2005 as domestic revenues
improved and aid grants covered most expenditures. Public debt fell dramatically to ~206%
of GDP as major debt relief deals were completed. The Paris Club granted substantial
debt relief in 2006 based on the HIPC initiative framework.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
A small fiscal surplus of 0.64% of GDP was achieved in 2006, partly due to aid-financed
spending covering most costs. Most significantly, public debt collapsed to ~23% of GDP
as the Paris Club granted massive debt relief in 2006. Afghanistan's fiscal position
transformed from extreme debt distress to relative sustainability in a single year.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
A fiscal deficit of -2.5% of GDP occurred in 2007 as security spending increased with
the intensifying insurgency. Debt remained at ~20% of GDP, a sustainable level post-relief.
The government continued to rely heavily on donor funding for both operating and development
expenditures.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Fiscal deficit widened to -3.9% of GDP in 2008 as security spending rose with the
intensifying conflict. Debt at ~19% of GDP remained manageable. However, government
revenue coverage of expenditures remained very low; the government could not finance its
operating budget from domestic resources alone.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Fiscal deficit narrowed to -1.8% of GDP in 2009 as aid inflows increased with the US
surge. Debt fell to ~16% of GDP. Government domestic revenues were growing slowly.
However, the fiscal position was entirely dependent on continued donor support.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
A small fiscal surplus of 0.9% of GDP in 2010 as aid grants more than covered government
expenditures. Debt fell to only 7.7% of GDP. However, the Kabul Bank crisis erupted,
revealing systemic corruption and potentially creating significant contingent liabilities
for the government.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Modest fiscal deficit of -0.7% of GDP in 2011 with debt at 7.5% of GDP, near the lowest
level in the dataset. Nominal fiscal health appeared strong by standard indicators, but
this masked total aid-dependency: the government could not finance its operations from
domestic revenues and was entirely dependent on continuing donor grants.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
Near-zero fiscal balance in 2012 with debt at 6.8% of GDP. Domestic revenue collection
was improving, but the economy faced increasing headwinds as international military presence
began winding down, threatening aid flows that underpinned fiscal stability.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
Small fiscal deficit of -0.6% of GDP in 2013 with debt at 6.9% of GDP. As the transition
year approached, concerns about fiscal sustainability post-drawdown intensified. Domestic
revenues were growing but far from sufficient to sustain government operations without
international support.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
Fiscal deficit widened to -1.7% of GDP in 2014 as international troops withdrew and
economic activity contracted. Debt rose to 8.7% of GDP but remained manageable. The
fundamental fiscal fragility was exposed as domestic revenue growth slowed and donor
commitments became less certain.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
Fiscal deficit at -1.4% of GDP in 2015 with debt at 9.2% of GDP. The post-transition
security deterioration increased defense spending demands, while falling aid revenues
squeezed the budget. The National Unity Government struggled with fiscal management.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
Near-zero fiscal balance in 2016 with debt at 8.4% of GDP. Fiscal consolidation was
supported by IMF program engagement. Revenue mobilization improved but the underlying
aid-dependence remained. Security expenditures were a growing burden.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
Small fiscal deficit of -0.7% of GDP in 2017 with debt at 8.0% of GDP. IMF program
engagement continued and domestic revenues showed some improvement. The US extended its
military mission providing continued support, but fiscal sustainability remained dependent
on donors.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
A small fiscal surplus of 1.6% of GDP in 2018, with debt at 7.4% of GDP. Revenue
mobilization showed improvement under IMF program support. Despite nominal fiscal health,
the government remained entirely dependent on international grants and military assistance.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
Modest fiscal deficit of -1.1% of GDP in 2019 with debt at 6.4% of GDP, the lowest
in the dataset. Domestic revenues continued gradual improvement. The Doha peace process
raised uncertainty about future aid flows and fiscal sustainability.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Fiscal deficit widened to -2.2% of GDP in 2020 as COVID-19 hit revenues and increased
health spending. Debt rose to 7.8% of GDP. The Doha Agreement's troop withdrawal timeline
increased uncertainty about future military aid and civilian assistance.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGGGBGDPGDPPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
The Taliban takeover in August 2021 caused fiscal collapse. The central bank's $9.5 billion
in foreign reserves held in the US were frozen. International aid was suspended. The IMF
suspended engagement with Afghanistan. The government lost access to international financial
systems, payroll systems collapsed, and the fiscal framework effectively ceased to function
in recognizable form.

sources.citation: IMF, Afghanistan country page, 2021 Article IV consultation suspended

1996.assessment: No reliable GDP per capita estimate for 1996. Taliban rule (1996-2001) severed Afghanistan from international economic institutions. World Bank, IMF, and UN agencies had minimal access. Economy was largely informal/subsistence with severe drought and civil war damage.

1997.assessment: No reliable GDP per capita estimate for 1997. Taliban rule (1996-2001) severed Afghanistan from international economic institutions. World Bank, IMF, and UN agencies had minimal access. Economy was largely informal/subsistence with severe drought and civil war damage.

1998.assessment: No reliable GDP per capita estimate for 1998. Taliban rule (1996-2001) severed Afghanistan from international economic institutions. World Bank, IMF, and UN agencies had minimal access. Economy was largely informal/subsistence with severe drought and civil war damage.

1999.assessment: No reliable GDP per capita estimate for 1999. Taliban rule (1996-2001) severed Afghanistan from international economic institutions. World Bank, IMF, and UN agencies had minimal access. Economy was largely informal/subsistence with severe drought and civil war damage.

2000.assessment:
Afghanistan in 2000 was under Taliban rule with an extremely impoverished economy.
Prolonged civil conflict had destroyed infrastructure and agricultural output. The country
ranked among the world's poorest, with almost no formal economic activity beyond subsistence
agriculture and opium poppy cultivation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2001.assessment:
GDP per capita fell sharply in 2001, the year of the US-led invasion and Taliban overthrow.
The combination of severe drought, pre-existing conflict, and the disruption of the military
campaign collapsed already minimal economic activity. Afghanistan was one of the world's
poorest countries, with living standards at an extreme nadir.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2002.assessment:
The first full year of post-Taliban reconstruction saw modest GDP recovery as international
aid began flowing in and millions of refugees returned. However, GDP per capita remained
extremely low, reflecting the deep impoverishment from decades of conflict. The Bonn Agreement
had established an interim government and international support was ramping up rapidly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
GDP per capita continued a gradual upward trend in 2003, driven by reconstruction aid
and the formation of the transitional government. Agriculture began recovering from the
2001-2002 drought. Despite growth, Afghanistan remained among the world's poorest nations,
far below any regional benchmark.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $222 in 2004, the year of Afghanistan's first presidential election.
International aid continued to drive growth, and the security situation allowed some
economic activity in urban areas. However, rural areas remained deeply impoverished and
insurgency was beginning to resurge in the south.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $254 in 2005, continuing a post-2001 growth trajectory fueled by
aid inflows, reconstruction spending, and a growing service sector in Kabul. Parliamentary
elections were held. Despite macroeconomic growth, widespread poverty persisted and the
country remained heavily aid-dependent.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $274 in 2006, yet this growth masked deep structural weaknesses.
The Taliban insurgency intensified significantly in the south, threatening development
gains. Opium poppy cultivation expanded. NATO took command of ISAF, expanding from the
south to all of Afghanistan.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
A significant jump in GDP per capita to $376 occurred in 2007, partly reflecting increased
aid flows and continued reconstruction spending. However, the security situation deteriorated
markedly, with suicide bombings and IED attacks rising sharply. The gains remained
concentrated in Kabul and other major cities.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
GDP per capita was essentially flat in 2008 at $382, as continued conflict and global
commodity price shocks offset reconstruction spending. Violence reached levels not seen
since 2001. Food prices spiked globally, hitting the most vulnerable Afghans hardest.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
GDP per capita jumped to $452 in 2009 as the Obama administration significantly increased
aid and military spending. Presidential elections were held amid fraud controversies.
Despite macroeconomic growth figures, the conflict continued to intensify with a major
US-NATO surge announced at year-end.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $561 in 2010, near the period peak, driven by the military surge
bringing massive US spending. The Kabul Bank crisis erupted, revealing systemic corruption.
The Lisbon Summit established a transition timeline for Afghan security forces to take over.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $607 in 2011, close to the dataset peak, reflecting peak US/NATO
military and aid spending. The killing of Osama bin Laden occurred in Pakistan. The transition
to Afghan security forces began. Economic activity remained heavily dependent on international
military and aid presence.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita peaked at $651 in 2012, the highest recorded for Afghanistan in the dataset.
This peak reflected maximum international spending before troop drawdown. Despite strong
nominal figures, growth was unsustainable, driven by donor-funded activity rather than
productive economic development.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita declined slightly to $637 in 2013 as the drawdown of US forces began reducing
aid-driven economic activity. Growth uncertainty increased as the 2014 security transition
approached and international spending started contracting.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to $625 in 2014 as US/NATO combat operations formally ended and
international military presence sharply reduced. The disputed presidential election
and political uncertainty further dampened economic confidence. Significant aid-dependency
risk materialized as donor fatigue grew.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita dropped sharply to $566 in 2015 as the post-drawdown economic contraction
deepened. Security deteriorated as the Taliban captured Kunduz city for the first time.
Investment collapsed and capital flight accelerated. The National Unity Government struggled
to stabilize the economy amid ongoing conflict.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
GDP per capita continued declining to $522 in 2016. Conflict-related disruption to agriculture
and trade, combined with declining aid spending, weighed heavily on living standards. The
Taliban extended territorial gains while poppy cultivation reached record highs. Economic
activity concentrated increasingly in urban areas.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita stabilized slightly at $525 in 2017. The US extended its military mission
under the Trump administration's South Asia strategy. Conflict remained intense, with IS-K
emerging as a new threat alongside the Taliban insurgency. Economic stagnation persisted.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to $491 in 2018 as conflict intensified and US-Taliban peace talks began.
Security remained dire across much of the country. The economy was highly dependent on
external aid, which showed signs of further decline. Drought also affected agricultural
output and drove internal displacement.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita was roughly flat at $497 in 2019. The Doha peace process continued with
US-Taliban negotiations. Presidential elections were contested. Aid-dependence remained
extreme and the Taliban controlled or contested roughly half the country's districts.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
GDP per capita rose slightly to $511 in 2020 despite the COVID-19 pandemic. The Doha
Agreement was signed in February 2020, setting a US withdrawal timeline. Violence initially
fell under the agreement terms but intra-Afghan peace negotiations stalled. COVID-19
exacerbated humanitarian conditions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita collapsed to $357 in 2021 following the Taliban's rapid takeover in August.
The US withdrew all forces, the Ghani government collapsed, and the Taliban seized Kabul.
International aid was suspended, the central bank's foreign reserves were frozen, and
the banking system was paralyzed. This was the sharpest single-year economic contraction
in recent Afghan history.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
GDP per capita remained at the collapsed level of $357 in 2022 under Taliban rule.
International isolation, sanctions, and frozen assets perpetuated the economic crisis.
Humanitarian catastrophe deepened with widespread food insecurity. Taliban governance
excluded women from the economy and education, further reducing productive capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita showed modest nominal recovery to $414 in 2023, but this may partly reflect
currency effects rather than real improvements. Afghanistan remained in deep humanitarian
crisis under Taliban rule, with no international recognition of the government and
continued restrictions on women's work and education.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NY.GDP.PCAP.CD, via FRED PCAGDPAFA646NWDB, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment: GDP per capita for 2024 not yet published by World Bank. Under Taliban administration since 2021, economic data collection severely limited. IMF suspended regular Article IV consultations.

2025.assessment: GDP per capita for 2025 not yet published by World Bank. Under Taliban administration since 2021, economic data collection severely limited. IMF suspended regular Article IV consultations.

1997.assessment: No official CPI or inflation data for 1997. Afghanistan under Taliban rule had no functioning central bank statistics. IMF had no Article IV consultation. Severe hyperinflation occurred but was unmeasured.

2001.assessment: No official CPI or inflation data for 2001. Afghanistan under Taliban rule had no functioning central bank statistics. IMF had no Article IV consultation. Severe hyperinflation occurred but was unmeasured.

2002.assessment:
Inflation was modest at 5.1% in 2002, the first year of recorded data after the Taliban
period. The new transitional government began establishing a central bank and monetary
framework. Currency reform and the introduction of the new afghani helped stabilize
price expectations.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
Inflation surged to 35.7% in 2003, reflecting post-conflict monetary instability, massive
aid inflows creating demand pressures, and supply constraints from years of disrupted
agriculture and trade. The new afghani currency was introduced in late 2002 as part of
currency reform, and price adjustments were significant in 2003.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
Inflation fell to 16.4% in 2004, indicating partial stabilization after the currency
transition. Agricultural recovery helped reduce food price pressures. Despite declining,
inflation remained elevated above target levels with continued supply-side constraints
and reconstruction demand.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
Inflation declined to 10.6% in 2005, approaching single digits as Da Afghanistan Bank
implemented tighter monetary policy. Good agricultural harvests eased food price pressures.
The economy was stabilizing, though inflation remained above optimal levels.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
Inflation dropped to 6.8% in 2006, a period of relative monetary stability. Da Afghanistan
Bank's monetary framework was functioning. However, insurgency-driven supply disruptions
in southern provinces kept some price pressures elevated.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Inflation rose to 8.7% in 2007 as global commodity prices began rising and the insurgency
disrupted supply chains more significantly. Conflict in the south and east affected
agricultural production and transport networks, contributing to food price increases.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Inflation surged to 26.4% in 2008, driven by the global food and fuel price crisis.
Afghanistan as a heavily import-dependent economy was severely affected. Food prices
spiked dramatically, causing humanitarian distress particularly in urban areas and among
the rural poor. This was the highest inflation since 2003.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Deflation of -6.8% occurred in 2009, largely as a reversal of the 2008 commodity price
spike. Global food and fuel prices fell sharply, dragging down Afghan prices. The deflationary
period was a base effect rather than a sign of economic contraction, as the economy
continued growing in nominal terms driven by aid spending.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
Inflation returned to a low 2.2% in 2010 after the prior year's deflation. Price stability
was maintained as the central bank kept its framework and global commodity prices were
relatively stable. This was one of the more favorable inflation readings in Afghanistan's
post-2001 history.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Inflation rebounded to 11.8% in 2011 as the global commodity price surge hit Afghanistan.
Food prices rose significantly. Massive US military spending also contributed to demand-pull
inflation in major cities. The Kabul Bank crisis of 2010-2011 added economic uncertainty.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 6.4% in 2012 as prices stabilized. The transition from NATO combat
operations continued and economic activity remained relatively stable supported by continued
aid. No major commodity shocks occurred.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
Inflation at 7.4% in 2013 reflected modest price pressures. Uncertainty about the post-NATO
security environment began affecting economic confidence. The transition year approached
with increasing uncertainty.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
Inflation fell to 4.7% in 2014 as the post-NATO transition occurred and economic activity
slowed. Reduced demand from departing military forces and falling commodity prices globally
helped contain inflation. Economic uncertainty increased as security deteriorated.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
Mild deflation of -0.7% occurred in 2015, reflecting declining demand as international
spending dropped sharply post-transition. Global commodity price falls, especially fuel,
contributed. The economy was contracting and deflation masked severe economic distress.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
Inflation returned to 4.4% in 2016 as commodity prices recovered slightly and the
economy adjusted to lower international spending levels. Security continued to deteriorate
with Taliban territorial gains.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
Inflation at 5.0% in 2017 was relatively moderate. The US extended its mission with
increased troop presence providing some economic stimulus. Continued conflict disrupted
supply chains in contested areas but urban economic activity was relatively stable.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
Inflation was very low at 0.6% in 2018 as economic stagnation and drought reduced demand.
Drought severely affected agricultural output while conflict restricted supply. Near-zero
inflation masked distress rather than reflecting healthy price stability.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
Inflation remained low at 2.3% in 2019 as the economy stagnated and peace talks progressed.
Low global commodity prices helped contain inflation. Restrained economic activity from
ongoing conflict and political uncertainty kept prices in check.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Inflation rose to 5.6% in 2020 as COVID-19 disrupted supply chains and the Doha Agreement
set a US withdrawal timeline. Supply disruptions from pandemic-related trade restrictions
and continued conflict contributed to moderate price increases.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
Inflation of 7.8% in 2021 reflects the annual average including the economic shock of
the Taliban takeover. The collapse of the banking system, aid suspension, and severe
supply disruptions drove food and fuel prices sharply higher in the second half of 2021.
By late 2021, food insecurity was extreme and WHO declared a humanitarian emergency.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Inflation rose to 10.6% in 2022 as the Taliban-era economic crisis deepened. Global food
price inflation from the Russia-Ukraine war compounded already severe domestic supply
disruptions. Aid restrictions and banking system dysfunction perpetuated economic hardship.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
Deflation of -7.7% was recorded in 2023, likely reflecting severe demand collapse under
the Taliban-era economic crisis. With incomes collapsed, purchasing power destroyed, and
the banking system dysfunctional, consumer demand fell sharply. This deflation reflects
economic catastrophe rather than any positive stabilization.

sources.citation: Worlddata.info, Afghanistan Inflation Rates, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment:
Continued deflation of -4.3% in 2024 under Taliban rule reflects persistent demand
destruction and economic collapse. The humanitarian crisis continued with widespread food
insecurity. International isolation, asset freezes, and exclusion of women from the
workforce severely constrained economic activity.

sources.citation: IMF Regional Economic Outlook, AFGPCPIPCHPT via FRED, accessed 2026-03-01

2025.assessment: 2025 inflation data not yet released by IMF WEO. Taliban-era economic data remains unreliable.

1996.assessment:
Taliban assumed power in Kabul in 1996. No reliable national accounts or trade statistics
available. Afghanistan was heavily sanctioned and isolated. Trade consisted primarily of
opium exports, cross-border goods smuggling, and some transit trade. Economy extremely
restricted. No formal trade data published by any international institution for this period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — no Afghanistan trade data for 1990s Taliban era

1997.assessment:
Taliban controlled most of Afghanistan. UN sanctions imposed November 1999 (retroactive
context). Trade severely restricted. No formal trade statistics available. Economy
dominated by opium, informal cross-border trade. Restricted trade openness estimated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — no Afghanistan trade data for Taliban era

1998.assessment:
Taliban consolidated control of Afghanistan in 1998 (capturing Mazar-i-Sharif in August).
US air strikes following embassy bombings. Very limited formal trade. No trade data published.
Restricted trade openness estimated based on political economy context.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — no Afghanistan trade data for Taliban era

1999.assessment:
Taliban rule continuing. UN Security Council Resolution 1267 imposed targeted sanctions
in October 1999. Taliban ordered opium ban in this period. No formal trade data available.
Restricted formal trade openness estimated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — no Afghanistan trade data for Taliban era; UNSC 1267

2000.assessment:
Taliban complete opium ban effective 2000-2001. UNSC Resolution 1333 additional sanctions
December 2000. Economy severely contracted. No formal trade statistics. Humanitarian
imports and some cross-border smuggling only. Restricted trade openness estimated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — no Afghanistan trade data; UNSC 1333 2000

2001.assessment:
US-led invasion began October 7, 2001 following September 11 attacks. Taliban collapsed
by December 2001; Bonn Agreement December 5. Massive economic disruption during conflict.
No reliable formal trade data for 2001. Post-invasion humanitarian imports begin. Transition
from closed Taliban economy to opening. Restricted to minimal trade estimated.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI — no Afghanistan trade data for 2001 invasion year

2002.assessment:
After the fall of the Taliban, trade opened rapidly as Afghanistan reconnected with
international markets. Imports were particularly high as aid and reconstruction goods
flooded in. Reports suggest trade as a share of GDP was very high in 2002-2003,
possibly exceeding 100% due to massive import of aid goods relative to very small GDP.

sources.citation: World Bank WITS, Afghanistan Trade in % of GDP, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
Trade openness was reported at very high levels in 2003, with some estimates suggesting
trade (exports + imports) reached 137% of GDP, reflecting the massive inflow of aid goods
and reconstruction materials relative to Afghanistan's small formal GDP. Afghanistan was
highly aid-import dependent.

sources.citation: World Bank analysis cited in academic literature on Afghan trade openness

2004.assessment:
Trade remained very high relative to GDP as international aid flows sustained extremely
high import levels. Afghanistan's own exports were minimal (dried fruit, carpets, opium),
while imports included food, fuel, manufactured goods, and military supplies.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
Trade openness remained very high in 2005 with import-driven economy. Aid inflows
continued at high levels. The formal Afghan economy was extremely small relative to
trade volumes, making Afghanistan among the most aid-import dependent countries globally.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
Trade openness declined to 85% of GDP in 2006 from the very high levels of 2002-2005.
As the economy grew (larger denominator), trade as a share of GDP naturally fell, though
trade volumes remained high. Afghanistan remained heavily import-dependent, with trade
dominated by aid goods, fuel, food, and military supplies.

sources.citation: FRED, Openness at constant prices for Afghanistan (OPENRPAFA156NUPN), accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Trade openness was 77% of GDP in 2007, declining as the economy grew faster than trade
volumes. Security deterioration in the south began affecting cross-border trade routes
through Pakistan (Torkham and Spin Boldak), though overall trade remained high.

sources.citation: FRED, Openness at constant prices for Afghanistan (OPENRPAFA156NUPN), accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Trade openness at 76% of GDP in 2008, relatively stable from 2007. Global commodity
price spikes increased import costs, particularly for food and fuel. Trade volumes in
nominal terms increased, but the ratio to GDP remained roughly constant.

sources.citation: FRED, Openness at constant prices for Afghanistan (OPENRPAFA156NUPN), accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Trade openness declined to 67% of GDP in 2009 as the economy continued growing while
trade growth slowed. Commodity price deflation reduced import values in nominal terms.
Security challenges in the south and east continued to disrupt trade corridors.

sources.citation: FRED, Openness at constant prices for Afghanistan (OPENRPAFA156NUPN), accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
Trade openness fell to 57% of GDP in 2010 as GDP growth (surge-driven) outpaced trade
growth. Last year of FRED data series. Afghanistan remained import-dependent, but the
growing formal economy reduced the trade-to-GDP ratio. Security improvements in some
areas enabled some trade expansion.

sources.citation: FRED, Openness at constant prices for Afghanistan (OPENRPAFA156NUPN), accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Trade openness likely continued declining as a share of GDP as the economy was near
peak size. FRED series ends in 2010. Based on the trend, trade openness was approximately
50-60% of GDP in 2011. The NATO drawdown beginning would eventually reduce import-related
military supply chain trade.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
FRED OPENRPAFA series ends at 2010. World Bank WDI data for Afghanistan trade openness
is incomplete for 2012-2020. Based on the declining trend from 57% in 2010, trade openness
likely declined further as the formal economy shrank while aid flows also reduced. ISAF
surge period (2009-2012) provided some economic stimulus but trade-to-GDP ratio likely
continued declining. Estimated moderate trade openness (40-60% of GDP).

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade openness trend analysis

2013.assessment:
ISAF drawdown beginning. Economic aid declining. Trade openness likely moderate,
estimated between 40-60% of GDP. Security deterioration began reducing trade activity
in some provinces. Import-dependent economy with declining aid flows.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade openness trend; ISAF drawdown context

2014.assessment:
ISAF ended December 2014; Resolute Support Mission began. NUG formation. Aid flows
declining. Trade openness estimated moderate. Economy contracting as security deteriorated
and military supply chains withdrew. Trade-to-GDP ratio likely near 40-50%.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan; ISAF end December 2014

2015.assessment:
Post-ISAF period. Aid-driven imports declining. Security deterioration affecting trade
corridors. Economy contracting. Trade openness estimated moderate (approximately 40-55%
of GDP), declining from surge era peaks.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade trend post-ISAF

2016.assessment:
Security deterioration significant. Taliban taking rural districts. Aid flows continued
declining. Economy contracted. Trade openness estimated moderate (40-55% of GDP).
Import-dependent economy with declining aid financing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade; security deterioration 2016

2017.assessment:
US South Asia strategy announced 2017 with renewed commitment. Aid flows partially
stabilizing. Trade openness estimated moderate (40-55% of GDP). Economy fragile but
import-dependent trade maintained through Kabul-Pakistan corridor.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade; US South Asia strategy 2017

2018.assessment:
Doha peace negotiations beginning. Economy fragile. Trade openness estimated moderate
(40-55% of GDP). Import-dependent; exports minimal (dried fruit, carpets, some minerals).
Kabul-Pakistan and Kabul-Iran trade corridors operational.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade; Doha negotiations 2018

2019.assessment:
Pre-Doha Agreement. Trade openness estimated moderate (40-60% of GDP). Some WITS data
suggests Afghanistan maintained moderate import levels financed by aid and remittances.
Mineral exports beginning to grow. Economy fragile but formal trade maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade openness 2019; WITS country snapshot

2020.assessment:
Doha Agreement signed February 2020. COVID-19 disrupted border crossings and trade
logistics. Trade openness estimated moderate but declining due to COVID disruptions.
Estimated 40-55% of GDP. Aid flows maintained but border closures temporarily
disrupted imports.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Afghanistan trade 2020; Doha Agreement; COVID border closures

2021.assessment:
Trade collapsed in 2021 following the Taliban takeover. International trade routes
were disrupted, banking systems failed, and import financing dried up. Afghanistan
became severely isolated economically. WITS data for 2023 shows imports at 50.7% and
exports at 16.9% of GDP, suggesting total trade of approximately 67% of GDP in later
Taliban years, but 2021 would have been much lower mid-collapse.

sources.citation: WITS Afghanistan country snapshot, 2023 figures, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Trade recovering from 2021 collapse but remained severely depressed. WITS 2023 data
suggests trade openness recovered to approximately 67% of GDP by 2023. For 2022,
cross-border trade with Pakistan, Iran, and Central Asia partially resumed but
banking system failures continued to impede normal trade financing. Estimated
moderate trade openness (40-60% of GDP) based on 2023 WITS data trajectory.

sources.citation: WITS Afghanistan country snapshot 2023, accessed 2026-03-01; extrapolated to 2022

2023.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 67.6% of GDP in 2023 based on WITS data showing
imports at 50.7% and exports at 16.9% of GDP. Afghanistan under Taliban rule maintained
some cross-border trade with neighboring countries (Pakistan, Iran, Central Asia) but
overall trade volumes were constrained by the economic crisis and international isolation.

sources.citation: WITS Afghanistan country snapshot, 2023, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment:
Based on 2023 WITS data (67.6% GDP), trade openness continued at moderate level in 2024.
Taliban cross-border trade with Pakistan and Iran maintained. No significant changes to
trade policy. Banking and payment system constraints continued limiting formal trade.
Estimated moderate trade openness (approximately 60-70% of GDP).

sources.citation: WITS Afghanistan 2023 data extrapolated for 2024; World Bank

2025.assessment:
Trade openness estimated to continue at moderate level based on 2023 WITS data trajectory.
Taliban governance has stabilized some cross-border trade flows despite banking system
constraints. Opium trade banned by Taliban (2022 edict). Mineral exports increasing.
Estimated moderate trade openness (60-70% of GDP).

2002.assessment:
Unemployment in post-Taliban Afghanistan was difficult to measure formally, as most
economic activity was informal. ILO modeled estimates for Afghanistan show unemployment
in the low double digits historically, but the immediate post-Taliban period had very
high underemployment and informal sector dominance. The formal labor market barely existed.

sources.citation: ILO modeled estimates via World Bank WDI SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Afghanistan

2003.assessment:
The ILO modeled unemployment estimate for Afghanistan is approximately 8% in the early
post-Taliban reconstruction period. However, this figure is almost certainly a severe
underestimate of actual joblessness, as the formal labor market was nascent and most
Afghans worked in subsistence agriculture or informal activities. Underemployment and
poverty were far more widespread than formal unemployment statistics suggest.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate), Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
ILO modeled unemployment estimate of approximately 7.9% in 2004. Presidential elections
created some formal sector employment. However, the bulk of the population remained in
subsistence agriculture or informal activity. Drug economy (poppy cultivation) provided
livelihoods for a significant portion of rural labor.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate), Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
Unemployment remained in the 7-8% range per ILO modeled estimates. Parliamentary elections
held. Reconstruction created formal sector jobs in urban areas. Rural livelihoods remained
dominated by agriculture, with growing poppy cultivation providing incomes in insecure areas.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate), Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 7.9% per ILO modeled estimates. The insurgency's intensification
in the south began displacing agricultural workers and reducing economic activity in
conflict-affected areas. Aid-sector jobs in Kabul partially offset losses elsewhere.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate), Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Unemployment around 7.9% per ILO modeled estimates. The formal unemployment figures mask
massive underemployment and conflict-driven displacement. NRVA surveys suggest actual
underemployment was much more widespread. Security deterioration in 2007 reduced
agricultural and construction employment in conflict zones.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate), Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Unemployment approximately 7.9% per ILO modeled estimates in 2008. The global food
price crisis raised food insecurity but did not dramatically change formal unemployment.
The conflict's intensification continued to affect livelihoods in southern provinces.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (ILO modeled estimate), Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Unemployment reached a low of approximately 7.8% in 2009 per ILO modeled estimates,
as the massive US troop surge created construction, logistics, and services employment.
However, the surge also displaced rural populations, creating a paradox of formal job
creation alongside increased vulnerability.

Unemployment at approximately 7.8% in 2010, near the ILO modeled low for Afghanistan.
US surge at its peak, with maximum foreign employment stimulus. Aid sector and military

Unemployment approximately 7.9% in 2011 as surge employment began to fade but international

Unemployment rose sharply to approximately 11% in 2020 as COVID-19 devastated the
informal economy and reduced international aid-related employment. The economic shock

2021.assessment:
Unemployment spiked dramatically to approximately 14% in 2021 following the Taliban
takeover. The collapse of the previous government, suspension of aid, banking system
freeze, and mass exodus of skilled workers and aid workers devastated formal employment.
Women were largely excluded from the workforce under Taliban decrees.

1996.assessment:
Taliban captured Kabul in September 1996. The UN continued recognizing the Rabbani
government (Northern Alliance) as the legitimate government of Afghanistan. Only
Pakistan recognized the Taliban in 1996. Saudi Arabia and UAE would follow in 1997.
Afghanistan remained a UN member under the Rabbani-held UN seat. International
isolation of Taliban-controlled territory was significant.

sources.citation: Rashid, Ahmed. 'Taliban.' Yale University Press, 2000.

1997.assessment:
Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and UAE formally recognized Taliban government — the only
three countries to do so. The UN General Assembly continued seating Rabbani's
Northern Alliance government. Taliban was barred from occupying Afghanistan's
UN seat. Taliban-controlled Afghanistan was functionally isolated from the
international diplomatic system with only three recognizing states.

sources.citation: Byman, Daniel. 'Deadly Connections: States that Sponsor Terrorism.' Cambridge University Press, 2005.

1998.assessment:
Taliban's recognition remained limited to Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and UAE. After
Al-Qaeda's August 1998 embassy bombings in Kenya and Tanzania, Saudi Arabia
suspended relations with Taliban in September 1998 (over refusal to extradite
Bin Laden). This reduced recognizing states to Pakistan and UAE. Taliban-controlled
Afghanistan effectively treated as a pariah.

sources.citation: US Embassy Bombings Report. 'Accountability Review Board Report.' 1999.

1999.assessment:
Taliban-controlled Afghanistan recognized only by Pakistan and UAE. UNSC 1267
imposed sanctions, treating Taliban as a pariah actor internationally. UN
recognized Rabbani government. Taliban denied access to UN bodies. International
NGOs and aid organizations operated under difficult conditions. Afghanistan's
international diplomatic status was effectively that of an unrecognized state.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1267 (1999). S/RES/1267.

2000.assessment:
Taliban diplomatic isolation deepened. UNSC 1333 expanded sanctions. Pakistan and
UAE were sole recognizing states. The UN maintained Northern Alliance in Afghanistan's
seat. Taliban Afghanistan was excluded from international organizations, multilateral
development banks, and diplomatic circuits. International community treated Taliban
as illegitimate. Taliban attempted to engage but was rebuffed.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1333 (2000). S/RES/1333.

2001.assessment:
After September 11, Pakistan pressured to abandon Taliban support. UAE severed
Taliban relations in September 2001. Post-US invasion, Taliban regime collapsed.
Bonn Agreement December 2001 established internationally recognized Interim
Authority under Hamid Karzai. Afghanistan immediately reintegrated diplomatically —
embassies began reopening, Karzai government received recognition, UN seat
transitioned to new government. New Afghan state had rapid international integration.

sources.citation: Bonn Agreement. 'Agreement on Provisional Arrangements in Afghanistan.' December 5, 2001.

2002.assessment:
Afghanistan fully reintegrated into international diplomatic system. Tokyo
Reconstruction Conference January 2002 pledged $4.5 billion. Embassies from
US, UK, Germany, France, India, Japan, and dozens of countries reopened in Kabul.
Afghanistan rejoined World Bank, IMF, Asian Development Bank. UNSC active
engagement in post-conflict reconstruction. Afghanistan's international relations
fully normalized with the Karzai Transitional Authority.

sources.citation: Tokyo Reconstruction Conference for Afghanistan. 'Donor Pledges.' January 2002.

2003.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration continued. NATO took command of ISAF in August 2003.
Constitutional Loya Jirga held December 2003. Afghanistan increasingly integrated
into international conferences, multilateral institutions, and bilateral relationships.
Berlin Conference April 2004 pledged further reconstruction aid. Afghan government
participated in UN General Assembly, international forums.

sources.citation: Berlin Conference on Afghanistan. 'Donor Pledges and Agreements.' April 2004.

2004.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Presidential election in October 2004 — Karzai
elected — further legitimized Afghanistan internationally. Major diplomatic
presence in Kabul. US Embassy one of largest globally. Afghanistan participated
actively in UN, international organizations, bilateral relationships with dozens
of countries.

sources.citation: OSCE/ODIHR. 'Afghanistan Presidential Election 2004.' 2005.

2005.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Parliamentary elections September 2005 internationally
observed. London Conference February 2006 prepared. Afghanistan Compact framework
established. Multiple bilateral strategic partnerships signed. Afghanistan fully
integrated in international diplomatic system.

sources.citation: London Conference on Afghanistan. 'Afghanistan Compact.' February 2006.

2006.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. London Conference February 2006, Afghanistan Compact
signed. NATO ISAF expanded across all regions. Afghanistan participated fully in
international forums. Multiple embassies active in Kabul. Growing international
criticism of corruption and governance but diplomatic relations maintained.

sources.citation: London Conference on Afghanistan. 'Afghanistan Compact.' February 2006.

2007.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration continued. Rome Conference February 2007 on rule of
law. Afghan National Development Strategy prepared. International community
engagement strong. Security deterioration in south did not reduce diplomatic
integration. Afghanistan participated in regional diplomatic forums including
Shanghai Cooperation Organization observer status discussions.

sources.citation: Afghan National Development Strategy. Interim 2007.

2008.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Paris Conference June 2008 pledged $21 billion.
Afghanistan-Pakistan-US-UK Quadrilateral Group. Multiple multilateral frameworks
active. International criticism of governance growing. Security deterioration
in south and east notable but diplomatic integration unchanged.

sources.citation: Paris Conference on Afghanistan. 'Donor Pledges.' June 2008.

2009.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration despite electoral crisis. US-Afghan Strategic Partnership
negotiations. Obama surge decision. The 2009 election fraud crisis generated
intense international pressure but did not break diplomatic relations. Afghanistan
fully integrated in UN, multilateral bodies, bilateral relationships.

sources.citation: US-Afghanistan Strategic Partnership. 2009.

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Kabul Conference July 2010. NATO Lisbon Summit
November 2010 established transition timeline to Afghan security lead. ANSF
transition framework. Afghanistan actively engaged diplomatically. Multiple
bilateral strategic partnerships. UNSC, G8, and NATO all actively engaged.

sources.citation: Kabul Conference. 'Communique.' July 2010.

2011.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Bonn II Conference December 2011 — 10 years after
original Bonn — major international conference on Afghanistan transition. Strategic
Partnership Agreement with US under negotiation. Afghanistan-Pakistan talks.
Full engagement in international system.

sources.citation: Bonn II Conference. 'International Conference on Afghanistan.' December 2011.

2012.assessment:
US-Afghanistan Strategic Partnership Agreement signed May 2012 for post-2014
engagement. NATO Chicago Summit designated Afghanistan as Major Non-NATO Ally
equivalent. Tokyo Mutual Accountability Framework. Full diplomatic integration.
Heart of Asia Istanbul Process established regional framework.

sources.citation: US-Afghanistan Strategic Partnership Agreement. May 2, 2012.

2013.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Afghanistan formally took over security lead from
ISAF June 2013. Bilateral Security Agreement with US negotiations. Multiple
diplomatic frameworks active. Afghanistan fully engaged in international system
ahead of 2014 transition.

sources.citation: ISAF Security Transition. 'Afghan Security Forces Lead.' June 2013.

2014.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Bilateral Security Agreement with US signed September
2014. NATO Status of Forces Agreement. ISAF ended December 31, 2014. Resolute
Support Mission began January 2015. NUG established October 2014. International
community maintained full relations despite electoral crisis. London Conference
2014.

sources.citation: US-Afghanistan Bilateral Security Agreement. September 30, 2014.

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. NATO Resolute Support Mission active. Multiple
international conferences. NUG full diplomatic recognition. Heart of Asia
regional process. US Embassy, major European embassies, regional neighbors
fully engaged. UNAMA active. Mullah Omar death announcement did not change
diplomatic architecture.

sources.citation: NATO Resolute Support Mission. 'Status Update.' 2015.

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Brussels Conference October 2016 pledged $15.2 billion.
Afghanistan participated in G7 outreach, regional diplomatic forums, UN General
Assembly. Multiple bilateral strategic partnerships. Quadrilateral Coordination
Group with Pakistan, US, China on peace process.

sources.citation: Brussels Conference on Afghanistan. October 2016.

2017.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Trump South Asia Strategy announced August 2017
reaffirmed US commitment. NATO maintained Resolute Support. Multiple bilateral
strategic partnerships active. Afghanistan participated in UN, multilateral
frameworks. Full integration maintained.

sources.citation: Trump Administration. 'South Asia Strategy.' August 21, 2017.

2018.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Geneva Conference on Afghanistan November 2018
pledged continued support. US-Taliban Doha talks beginning. Afghan government
sought inclusion. Full bilateral relationships active. UNAMA, international
NGOs, diplomatic missions fully operational in Kabul.

sources.citation: Geneva Conference on Afghanistan. 'Communique.' November 2018.

2019.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. US-Taliban Doha negotiations progressed. Afghan
government marginalized from Doha talks but retained full international recognition
and relations. Multiple international conferences. Resolute Support maintained.
Afghanistan participated in UNGA, multilateral bodies.

sources.citation: US Department of State. 'Afghanistan Peace Process.' 2019.

2020.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. Doha Agreement signed February 29, 2020. Afghan
government-Taliban intra-Afghan talks began September 2020. International
community fully engaged. Multiple embassies active. UNAMA operational. Afghan
government maintained full bilateral relationships. US drawdown plans announced.

sources.citation: US-Taliban Doha Agreement. February 29, 2020.

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 15, 2021. All Western embassies evacuated by August 31.
No country recognized the Taliban Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan. The UN did not
recognize Taliban government. Afghan UN seat contested — Taliban requested seat
but UNSC credentials committee deferred decision. Afghanistan's former government
representatives remained in Afghan embassies abroad for months then were displaced.
Complete collapse of Afghanistan's diplomatic integration from the republic period.

sources.citation: UN General Assembly Credentials Committee. 'Deferral of Afghanistan Credentials.' 2021.
US Embassy Kabul. 'Embassy Closure August 31, 2021.' US Department of State.

2022.assessment:
No country recognized Taliban government. UN seat deferred. Some countries
maintained limited diplomatic presence (China, Russia, Pakistan, Iran, Turkey kept
missions open or maintained contact). Taliban participated in limited diplomatic
engagements (Doha talks) but with no recognition. Afghan embassies abroad gradually
switched to Taliban-appointed ambassadors or closed. UNAMA maintained presence.

sources.citation: UN Credentials Committee. 'Report to General Assembly.' 2022.

2023.assessment:
No country had recognized the Taliban government. UN credentials deferred for
third consecutive year. Taliban participated in UN-hosted Doha process meetings
on Afghanistan but without recognition. Women's rights restrictions (ban on girls'
secondary/university education) deepened international isolation. Multiple
countries suspended or curtailed diplomatic activities.

sources.citation: UN Special Envoy for Afghanistan. 'Doha Process.' 2023.

2024.assessment:
No country recognized Taliban government. UN credentials deferred. Taliban
participated in Doha process meetings with international special envoys.
Taliban continued engagement with China (Ambassador present), Russia, Pakistan,
Iran. UNSC maintained focus on Afghanistan. International community maintained
principled engagement without recognition. Taliban codified gender apartheid
policies further deepened isolation.

sources.citation: UN Special Envoy for Afghanistan. 'Doha Process Report.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
No country recognized Taliban government into 2025. Taliban continued Doha
process engagement. International community maintained humanitarian channel
while withholding recognition. Taliban's gender apartheid policy (Supreme Court
restrictions on women's movement, appearance, voice) precluded normalization.
Afghanistan remained diplomatically isolated with limited state-to-state
contact through Pakistan, China, Iran channels.

sources.citation: UN OCHA. 'Afghanistan Situation Report.' 2025.

1996.assessment: FDI data unavailable for 1996. Afghanistan under Taliban rule was subject to international sanctions and was effectively closed to foreign investment. UNCTAD records show no meaningful inward FDI. World Bank has no data for this period.

1997.assessment: FDI data unavailable for 1997. Afghanistan under Taliban rule was subject to international sanctions and was effectively closed to foreign investment. UNCTAD records show no meaningful inward FDI. World Bank has no data for this period.

1998.assessment: FDI data unavailable for 1998. Afghanistan under Taliban rule was subject to international sanctions and was effectively closed to foreign investment. UNCTAD records show no meaningful inward FDI. World Bank has no data for this period.

1999.assessment: FDI data unavailable for 1999. Afghanistan under Taliban rule was subject to international sanctions and was effectively closed to foreign investment. UNCTAD records show no meaningful inward FDI. World Bank has no data for this period.

2000.assessment: FDI data unavailable for 2000. Afghanistan under Taliban rule was subject to international sanctions and was effectively closed to foreign investment. UNCTAD records show no meaningful inward FDI. World Bank has no data for this period.

2001.assessment: FDI data unavailable for 2001. Afghanistan under Taliban rule was subject to international sanctions and was effectively closed to foreign investment. UNCTAD records show no meaningful inward FDI. World Bank has no data for this period.

2002.assessment:
FDI inflows were approximately 1.3% of GDP in 2002 as post-Taliban reconstruction
began following the Bonn Agreement and Tokyo Conference. Initial foreign investment
was primarily donor-driven reconstruction activity and some private sector returning.
The Afghan economy was extremely small making percentage-of-GDP figures relatively
large in absolute dollar terms. Investment climate severely limited by insecurity,
lack of legal framework, and infrastructure destruction.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.84% of GDP. Reconstruction activity ongoing but private
investor confidence limited. Telecoms sector (Roshan) beginning to attract
investment. Security deteriorating in south. Constitutional Loya Jirga underway.
Very low private FDI given conflict environment and weak legal/regulatory
framework.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.96% of GDP. Presidential election year generated some
investor optimism. Telecoms continued as main FDI recipient (Afghan Wireless,
Roshan). Mining sector attracted some interest. Overall investment climate
constrained by insecurity and limited regulatory framework.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
FDI reached a peak of approximately 4.36% of GDP in 2005 — the highest recorded
level for Afghanistan. This reflected post-election optimism, growing telecoms
sector, construction boom in Kabul, and early mining sector investment. Etisalat
and MTN entered the Afghan telecoms market. The relatively high FDI-to-GDP ratio
reflects both a small GDP base and genuine investment surge in reconstruction-
linked sectors. Parliamentary elections in September 2005 boosted confidence.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
FDI fell back to approximately 1.8% of GDP as security deteriorated sharply in
south following ISAF expansion. Taliban insurgency intensifying. London Conference
Afghanistan Compact provided framework but investor confidence declining. Telecoms
continued as main private sector FDI. Mining sector early-stage.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.6% of GDP. Taliban insurgency growing but reconstruction
investment continuing. Mining sector development (AIOC iron ore) attracted some
interest. Telecoms consolidated. Kabul construction sector maintained investment.
Security premium making investors cautious. Low FDI level overall.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.19% of GDP. Paris Conference pledged reconstruction aid but
private FDI remained low. Security deterioration across more provinces. Investment
climate constrained. Kabul-centric private investment continuing in construction,
retail, telecoms.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
FDI fell to approximately 0.72% of GDP. Election crisis and security deterioration
dampened investor confidence. Global financial crisis also reduced global FDI flows.
Obama surge beginning. Investment climate worsening. Very low private sector FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.54% of GDP. Security deterioration despite surge. Kabul
Bank scandal emerged (exposed 2010) severely damaged investor confidence in
financial sector. Mining sector Aynak copper mine Chinese investment (MCC)
stalled. Very low FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
FDI below 0.5% of GDP. Security conditions and post-Kabul Bank scandal damaged
investment climate. NATO drawdown announcement undermining business confidence.
Mining sector continued to stall. Minimal private FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.2% of GDP — continuing decline. Business confidence eroded
by insecurity and transition uncertainty. Aid levels beginning to plateau.
Investment climate weak. Minimal private FDI inflows.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.22% of GDP. Pre-election uncertainty. Aid-dependent economy
growing but private sector confidence low. Minimal private FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.29% of GDP. ISAF ended. NUG formation. Transition to
post-ISAF era created major business uncertainty. Aid reduction beginning.
Afghan economy contracted (GDP fell) as ISAF spending wound down. Minimal
FDI against deteriorating economic outlook.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.38% of GDP. Post-ISAF contraction continued. Kunduz briefly
fell to Taliban October 2015 — increased investor uncertainty. Aid-dependent
economy. Minimal private FDI against continued conflict and governance concerns.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.24% of GDP. Economy stagnant. Security deteriorating with
Taliban expanding control. Brussels Conference aid pledges but private confidence
low. Minimal FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.16% of GDP. Minimal. Trump South Asia Strategy extended
commitment but business confidence remained low. Security situation worsening.
Conflict economy dominant. Minimal private FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.11% of GDP. Record low. Doha peace talks beginning raised
some hope but business confidence remained depressed. Security at critical low.
Minimal FDI approaching zero.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.12% of GDP. Doha Agreement near completion but peace
uncertainty. Pre-election environment. Business confidence minimal. Minimal FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.06% of GDP — effectively near zero. COVID-19 impact on global
FDI flows plus Doha Agreement withdrawal timeline. Business uncertainty at maximum
given pending US withdrawal and Taliban talks. FDI near zero.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 2021 ended all meaningful FDI. DAB assets frozen.
International businesses evacuated or suspended operations. International
finance institutions suspended programs. FDI effectively negative as businesses
withdrew capital. World Bank FDI series for Afghanistan likely shows negative
or zero for 2021 given capital flight.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Afghanistan.

2022.assessment:
No meaningful FDI under Taliban rule. DAB assets frozen. No international
financial institution engagement. Banking system collapse. Foreign businesses
absent. Any remaining activity minimal/negative as capital continued to flee.
Negative net outflows effectively.

sources.citation: World Bank. 'Afghanistan Economic Monitor.' 2022.

2023.assessment:
No meaningful FDI. Taliban-ruled Afghanistan excluded from international financial
system. No World Bank programs. DAB assets frozen. Economy dependent on
humanitarian aid and informal cross-border trade. Negative or near-zero FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank. 'Afghanistan Development Update.' 2023.

2024.assessment:
No meaningful FDI. Afghanistan under Taliban remains outside international
financial system. DAB assets frozen. No foreign private investment. Some Chinese
and regional interest in mining sector (exploration only) but no committed
capital flows. Negative or zero FDI.

sources.citation: UN OCHA. 'Afghanistan Humanitarian Response Plan.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
No meaningful FDI. Afghanistan remains outside international financial system
under Taliban rule. Negative or zero net FDI. Economy dependent on humanitarian
transfers and informal trade.

sources.citation: UN OCHA. 'Afghanistan Situation Report.' 2025.

1996.assessment:
Pakistan's ISI provided direct support to Taliban, including advisors, materiel,
and strategic guidance during the Taliban's 1996 advance on Kabul. Pakistani
military volunteers and ISI personnel actively participated in combat operations.
Al-Qaeda fighters (Arab Afghans) fought alongside Taliban. Iran provided some
support to Northern Alliance factions. This constituted active foreign combat
involvement on both sides of the civil conflict.

sources.citation: Rashid, Ahmed. 'Taliban.' Yale University Press, 2000.
9/11 Commission Report. National Commission on Terrorist Attacks. 2004.

1997.assessment:
Pakistan ISI continued active support for Taliban military operations. Al-Qaeda
consolidated training camps with Pakistani acquiescence. Russia and Iran provided
materiel to Northern Alliance. Foreign military involvement from multiple state
and non-state actors characterized the civil war. All major parties had foreign
backers providing combat support.

sources.citation: Coll, Steve. 'Ghost Wars.' Penguin Press, 2004.

1998.assessment:
US launched cruise missile strikes against Al-Qaeda training camps in Khost,
Afghanistan on August 20, 1998 (Operation Infinite Reach) in response to
embassy bombings. Approximately 75 Tomahawk missiles fired. Pakistan ISI
continued Taliban support. Al-Qaeda operational in Afghanistan. Foreign combat
operations intensified with US strikes.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Operation Infinite Reach.' August 1998.

1999.assessment:
Pakistan ISI continued Taliban support. Al-Qaeda maintained training camp
network. CIA conducted surveillance and planning operations. Russia increased
support to Northern Alliance. UNSC 1267 sanctions imposed but foreign military
involvement continued. Active foreign military presence by multiple actors.

sources.citation: 9/11 Commission Report. National Commission on Terrorist Attacks. 2004.

2000.assessment:
Pakistan ISI maintained active Taliban support. Al-Qaeda training camp network
expanded. CIA Predator drone surveillance missions over Afghanistan began.
USS Cole bombing October 2000 increased US pressure on Taliban over Al-Qaeda.
Russia and Iran continued Northern Alliance support. Active foreign military
involvement by all parties continued.

sources.citation: Woodward, Bob. 'Bush at War.' Simon and Schuster, 2002.

2001.assessment:
US-led invasion began October 7, 2001 (Operation Enduring Freedom). US airpower
combined with Northern Alliance ground forces. UK, Canada, Australia participated.
CIA Jawbreaker teams on ground. Northern Alliance received US air support, enabling
rapid advance. Kabul fell November 13. Taliban collapsed by December. Peak foreign
combat involvement with coalition operations.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Operation Enduring Freedom.' 2001.
Woodward, Bob. 'Bush at War.' Simon and Schuster, 2002.

2002.assessment:
International Security Assistance Force (ISAF) established under UN mandate UNSC
1386 (December 2001), initially led by UK (approximately 4,650 troops). US Operation
Enduring Freedom continued combat operations against Taliban remnants in south and
east. Tora Bora and Anaconda operations. Coalition approximately 8,000-10,000 troops.
Forces invited by Afghan Interim Authority.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1386 (2001). ISAF mandate. S/RES/1386.

2003.assessment:
NATO took command of ISAF in August 2003. ISAF initially limited to Kabul region
(approximately 5,000 troops). OEF continued combat operations. Provincial
Reconstruction Teams deployed. US attention diverted to Iraq. Taliban insurgency
restarting in Helmand and Kandahar. Foreign forces invited presence conducting
both combat and reconstruction roles.

sources.citation: NATO. 'ISAF Mission.' 2003.

2004.assessment:
ISAF expanded beyond Kabul to northern regions. OEF combat operations continuing.
Combined US/ISAF forces approximately 15,000-18,000. Taliban insurgency growing.
Active combat operations in Helmand, Kandahar, Kunar provinces. Foreign forces
conducting both invited stabilization and active combat against insurgents.
ISAF expansion to west planned.

sources.citation: NATO ISAF. 'Status Update.' 2004.

2005.assessment:
ISAF expansion to western Afghanistan. OEF combat operations continued. Rising
Taliban attacks in south. Total coalition forces approximately 17,000-20,000.
Active combat operations in Kandahar, Helmand, Kunar. Foreign forces conducting
active combat against insurgency while invited by Afghan government.

sources.citation: NATO ISAF. 'Expansion to Western Afghanistan.' 2005.

2006.assessment:
ISAF expanded to southern Afghanistan in 2006 (Helmand, Kandahar, Uruzgan),
assuming combat role from US OEF. Operation Mountain Thrust (summer 2006) was
major combat operation. Total forces approximately 32,000-38,000. British forces
in Helmand, Canadians in Kandahar. Intense fighting with Taliban. Active foreign
combat operations at high intensity.

sources.citation: NATO ISAF. 'Stage 3 Expansion to Southern Afghanistan.' 2006.
UK Ministry of Defence. 'Operation Herrick 4 Report.' 2006.

2007.assessment:
ISAF full national coverage. Total NATO forces approximately 40,000-43,000 plus
US OEF approximately 15,000. Active combat operations across south and east.
IED attacks surging. International forces conducting active combat against
Taliban and associated groups.

sources.citation: NATO ISAF. 'Annual Report 2007.' 2008.

2008.assessment:
Active combat operations continued. US forces approximately 30,000-33,000. ISAF
approximately 50,000. Total coalition approximately 65,000-70,000. Taliban
conducting complex attacks including suicide bombings and Kabul Embassy attack.
Active combat operations at high intensity in south (Kandahar, Helmand) and east
(Kunar, Nuristan, Paktia).

sources.citation: NATO ISAF. 'Monthly Statistics.' 2008.

2009.assessment:
Obama surge announced December 2009 adding 30,000 additional US troops. US forces
grew from approximately 30,000 to approximately 68,000 by end 2009. Total coalition
approximately 100,000. Stanley McChrystal's COIN strategy. Active combat operations
across south and east Afghanistan at surge buildup intensity.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Report on Progress Toward Security and Stability in Afghanistan.' 2009.

2010.assessment:
Peak surge period. US forces reached approximately 100,000. Total coalition
approximately 132,000. Operation Moshtarak in Helmand (February 2010) was the
largest COIN operation of the war. Marjah and Kandahar operations. Active combat
operations at absolute peak with highest coalition casualties.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Afghanistan Progress Report.' 2010.

2011.assessment:
Surge still at peak. US approximately 90,000 troops, total coalition approximately
130,000. Bin Laden killed in Pakistan May 2011. NATO Lisbon Summit established
2014 transition timeline. Active combat operations continued at high intensity.
Beginning of drawdown planning but forces still at surge levels.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Report on Progress Toward Security and Stability in Afghanistan.' 2011.

2012.assessment:
Drawdown beginning. US forces declining from 100,000 peak toward 66,000 by end
2012. ANSF transition accelerating. Chicago Summit May 2012 accelerated transition.
Active combat operations continuing but foreign forces increasingly shifting to
advisory role in some areas.

sources.citation: NATO Chicago Summit. 'Chicago Communique.' May 2012.

2013.assessment:
ANSF assumed security lead across Afghanistan June 2013. US forces approximately
60,000-66,000. Transitioning from combat to advisory/assist role in most areas.
Active combat in east (Kunar, Nuristan). Total ISAF approximately 87,000. Still
active operations but shift toward advisory role underway.

sources.citation: ISAF. 'Security Transition Report.' 2013.

2014.assessment:
ISAF ended December 31, 2014. Resolute Support Mission (non-combat advisory)
began January 2015. US retained approximately 9,800 troops for RSM plus counter-
terrorism (Operation Freedom's Sentinel). In 2014 the drawdown completed from
approximately 38,000 to approximately 11,000 US troops. Year of major transition
from active combat to advisory mission.

sources.citation: NATO. 'ISAF Mission Ends / Resolute Support Begins.' 2014-2015.

2015.assessment:
NATO Resolute Support advisory mission approximately 12,000 troops. US Operation
Freedom's Sentinel approximately 9,800 troops (counter-terrorism mission retained).
These were invited forces conducting train/advise/assist and counter-terrorism.
Taliban captured Kunduz briefly October 2015 — US forces and airstrikes conducted
combat support. Significant invited forces with limited direct combat role.

sources.citation: NATO Resolute Support. 'Mission Update.' 2015.

2016.assessment:
Obama slowed withdrawal — retained approximately 8,400 US troops. NATO RSM
approximately 13,000 total. Invited forces in advisory/assist/counter-terrorism
role. US airstrikes against Taliban and IS-K continued. Significant invited forces
with active counter-terrorism component.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Afghanistan Progress Report.' 2016.

2017.assessment:
Trump South Asia Strategy August 2017 added approximately 3,000 US troops bringing
total US to approximately 14,000. NATO RSM total approximately 16,000. Significant
invited forces in advisory/counter-terrorism role. US airstrikes against Taliban
and IS-K significantly increased. MOAB dropped on IS-K in Nangarhar April 2017.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Afghanistan Progress Report.' 2017.

2018.assessment:
US approximately 14,000 troops. NATO RSM approximately 17,000 total. Invited
forces in advisory/counter-terrorism role. US airstrikes at record levels —
highest since 2010. Significant invited forces with active counter-terrorism
mission. Resolute Support advisory to ANSF corps level.

sources.citation: SIGAR. 'Quarterly Report to US Congress.' January 2019.

2019.assessment:
Doha negotiations with Taliban on troop withdrawal. US approximately 13,000-14,000
troops. NATO RSM approximately 17,000 total. Active counter-terrorism operations.
Doha Agreement signed February 2020 committed to full withdrawal within 14 months.
Significant invited forces maintained through year end.

sources.citation: SIGAR. 'Quarterly Report.' 2019.

2020.assessment:
Doha Agreement February 2020 committed US to withdraw all forces within 14 months.
US drawdown began — from approximately 12,000 to approximately 8,600 by November
2020, then approximately 2,500 by January 2021. NATO RSM reduced proportionally.
