Significant invited forces still present but declining substantially.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Doha Agreement Implementation.' 2020.

2021.assessment:
Biden announced full withdrawal by August 31, 2021. US forces approximately
2,500 in January, withdrawal accelerated April-August. Kabul fell August 15.
Emergency deployment approximately 6,000 US troops for evacuation (Operation
Allies Refuge). Final US military departure August 30, 2021. Full withdrawal
completed. All foreign military forces departed. Taliban assumed full security
control immediately.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Withdrawal from Afghanistan.' August 30, 2021.

2022.assessment:
No foreign military forces present in Afghanistan. Taliban Islamic Emirate
controlled security completely. US conducted over-the-horizon drone strike killing
Al-Zawahiri in Kabul August 2022 — unilateral, not invited. Otherwise no foreign
military presence. Taliban security forces deployed nationally.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Zawahiri Strike Statement.' August 1, 2022.

2023.assessment:
No foreign military forces present. Taliban security forces controlled all
Afghanistan. IS-K conducted attacks including against foreign interests. Taliban
conducted counter-IS-K operations. Pakistan conducted airstrikes against TTP
targets in eastern Afghanistan — uninvited and condemned by Taliban.

sources.citation: UN Analytical Support and Sanctions Monitoring Team. 'Report S/2023/370.' 2023.

2024.assessment:
No foreign military forces present. Taliban maintained full security sovereignty.
IS-K conducted attacks regionally. Pakistan-Afghanistan border tensions including
Pakistani strikes on TTP in Afghan territory. Taliban protested these as sovereignty
violations. No invited foreign military presence.

sources.citation: UN Monitoring Team. 'Report S/2024/92.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
No foreign military forces present. Taliban maintained full territorial security
control. IS-K continued regional operations. Taliban-Pakistan tensions over TTP
and border. No invited foreign military presence. Full Taliban sovereignty over
Afghan territory in security terms.

sources.citation: UN OCHA. 'Afghanistan Situation Report.' 2025.

1996.assessment:
Afghanistan had approximately 2.7-3 million refugees in Pakistan and Iran from
the Soviet-Afghan war era who had not returned. Taliban takeover of Kabul in
September 1996 generated additional displacement. UNHCR estimated 3-4 million
Afghan refugees externally and growing internal displacement from Taliban advances.
Significant ongoing outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'The State of the World's Refugees 1997.' Geneva.

1997.assessment:
Continued displacement from civil war between Taliban and Northern Alliance.
Approximately 2.6 million Afghan refugees in Pakistan, 1.4 million in Iran.
Fighting in northern Afghanistan generated additional displacement. Taliban
controlled most of country but Northern Alliance continued armed resistance.
Significant ongoing outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Situation.' 1997-1998.

1998.assessment:
Taliban capture of Mazar-i-Sharif in August 1998 generated massive additional
displacement. Hazara populations fled persecution. Total external refugees
estimated 3.5-4 million. Iran and Pakistan hosted the majority. Severe drought
beginning also contributed to internal displacement. Significant outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan: Civilians Flee Mazar Fighting.' 1998.

1999.assessment:
Severe drought continued generating displacement. Taliban controlled most of
Afghanistan. Approximately 3.5-4 million Afghan refugees externally. Internal
displacement worsened. Pakistan and Iran continued to host millions. Significant
outflows and static large refugee population.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Drought and Displacement.' 1999-2000.

2000.assessment:
Catastrophic drought (worst in 30 years) drove massive additional displacement.
UNHCR estimated approximately 3.6 million refugees outside Afghanistan and
approximately 1 million IDPs. Pakistan and Iran hosted the vast majority.
Taliban restricted humanitarian access. Significant ongoing outflows from
drought and continued conflict.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghan Drought Displacement 2000.' Geneva.

2001.assessment:
US-led invasion October 2001 generated additional displacement fears. Approximately
3.6-4 million Afghans still in exile. Internal displacement surged in anticipation
of bombing. However, by December 2001 with Taliban collapse, conditions created
for return. Pakistan and Iran hosted approximately 3.5 million. Significant outflows
during invasion period transitioning to return conditions at year end.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Operation.' 2001-2002.

2002.assessment:
Largest voluntary repatriation in UNHCR history. Approximately 1.8 million
Afghans returned in 2002 alone. From Pakistan approximately 1.5 million returned,
from Iran approximately 250,000. Spontaneous returns of millions began. Total
external refugee population began falling sharply. Net returns dominant despite
some ongoing displacement. Tokyo Conference reconstruction funds incentivized returns.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'The Largest Voluntary Repatriation in UNHCR History.' 2002.

2003.assessment:
Continued significant returns — approximately 400,000 in 2003. Total returns
since 2002 exceeded 2 million. Still approximately 2 million Afghans abroad
(Pakistan 1.4M, Iran 850K). Security in some areas generated some new outflows.
Net flows were still positive (returns exceeding new departures). Net returns
continued.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Repatriation Summary 2003.' Geneva.

2004.assessment:
Returns continued but at lower rate — approximately 350,000 in 2004. Growing
insurgency in south generated some new displacement. Net returns still positive
overall. Approximately 1.5-2 million still abroad. Presidential election year
created conditions for some returns but security south generating new IDP.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Repatriation 2004.' Geneva.

2005.assessment:
Returns declining — approximately 250,000 in 2005. Insurgency in south and east
generating new IDP. Mixed flows — some returning while others displaced by
conflict. Net returns still positive but increasingly mixed picture. Approximately
1.5 million still in Pakistan, 900,000 in Iran.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Returns 2005.' Geneva.

2006.assessment:
Return flow declining and new displacement increasing. ISAF southern expansion
triggered significant population displacement in Helmand and Kandahar. Growing
IDP population. Approximately 200,000 returned but conflict-generated outflows
growing. Transition from net returns to moderate outflows beginning.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement 2006.' Geneva.

2007.assessment:
Growing conflict-related displacement. Insurgency expanding to previously stable
provinces. IDP population growing in Afghanistan. External refugee flows continuing
to Pakistan and Iran but at lower rate than 2002-2005 peak. Approximately 150,000
returns. Growing IDP problem. Moderate outflows overall.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Situation 2007.' Geneva.

2008.assessment:
Moderate displacement. Pre-surge conflict intensity high. IDP numbers approximately
235,000. External refugees in Pakistan approximately 1.7 million, Iran approximately
900,000. Returns approximately 250,000. Mixed flows with moderate net outflows
from conflict provinces.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement Monitoring 2008.' Geneva.

2009.assessment:
Obama surge beginning. Conflict-related IDP grew to approximately 297,000.
External refugee population stabilizing (1.7M Pakistan, 1M Iran). Returns
approximately 100,000 (declining). Moderate outflows driven by intensifying
combat operations. Growing internal displacement alongside stable external
refugee population.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement 2009.' Geneva.

2010.assessment:
Peak surge year generated significant IDP — approximately 350,000. Operation
Moshtarak in Helmand displaced tens of thousands. External refugees approximately
2.9 million (Pakistan 1.9M, Iran 1M). Returns approximately 150,000. Net
displacement positive (more leaving than returning). Moderate outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Operation 2010.' Geneva.

2011.assessment:
Continuing moderate displacement. IDP approximately 450,000. External refugees
approximately 2.7 million. Returns approximately 130,000. Combat-related
displacement in south and east. Iran and Pakistan coercing some returns.
Moderate outflows with growing IDP population.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement 2011.' Geneva.

2012.assessment:
Moderate displacement continuing. Drawdown announcement affecting refugee
calculations. IDP approximately 487,000. External approximately 2.5 million.
Returns approximately 100,000. Persistent conflict-related displacement across
south and east. Moderate ongoing outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement Report 2012.' Geneva.

2013.assessment:
Moderate displacement. ANSF assumed security lead but Taliban activity continuing.
IDP approximately 631,000. External approximately 2.6 million. Decreasing returns.
Transition-related insecurity generating additional displacement. Moderate outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement 2013.' Geneva.

2014.assessment:
ISAF withdrawal generating displacement fears. IDP approximately 805,000. External
approximately 2.59 million. Returns approximately 80,000 (record low). Post-ISAF
insecurity concerns driving new displacement. Moderate outflows growing.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement 2014.' Geneva.

2015.assessment:
Significant displacement increase. IDP approximately 1.2 million — large increase.
Kunduz fell October 2015. Taliban gains increased displacement. Afghan asylum seekers
surged to Europe — Afghanistan second-largest nationality reaching Europe by sea in 2015
(behind Syria). External refugees approximately 2.7 million. Moderate outflows
trending upward toward significant.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Worldwide Displacement 2015.' Geneva.
IOM. 'Mixed Migration Flows into Europe.' 2015.

2016.assessment:
Continued high displacement. IDP approximately 1.2-1.5 million. EU-Turkey deal and
European border closures pushed Afghan migrants back. EU-Afghanistan cooperation
on returns. Pakistan expelled approximately 600,000 Afghans. External approximately
2.5 million. Moderate to significant outflows with forced returns complicating picture.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement Summary 2016.' Geneva.

2017.assessment:
Moderate displacement. IDP approximately 1.8 million — continued increase.
Pakistan and Iran continued pressuring returns. Taliban control expanding.
External approximately 2.5 million (Pakistan 1.4M, Iran 951K). Moderate
outflows with significant IDP.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement Report 2017.' Geneva.

2018.assessment:
Moderate displacement. IDP approximately 2 million — continued growth. Security
worsening with Taliban expanding control to urban centers (Ghazni attack). External
approximately 2.6 million. Returns approximately 60,000. Moderate outflows with
growing IDP and external refugees.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement Summary 2018.' Geneva.

2019.assessment:
Continued moderate displacement. IDP approximately 2.9 million (including conflict
and drought). External approximately 2.7 million. Limited returns. Doha Agreement
talks ongoing. Moderate outflows with significant IDP driven by both conflict
and climate/drought factors.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement 2019.' Geneva.

2020.assessment:
Moderate displacement. COVID-19 reduced cross-border movement initially. Doha
Agreement signed. IDP approximately 3.2 million. External approximately 2.6-2.7
million. COVID-related forced returns from Iran approximately 1 million in 2020.
Moderate outflows with massive COVID-related returns.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Situation 2020.' Geneva.

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 2021 generated mass displacement. Kabul airlift evacuated
approximately 120,000-124,000 people. Hundreds of thousands fled to Iran and
Pakistan across land borders — UNHCR estimated approximately 400,000-500,000
new refugees and asylum seekers in the following months. IDPs surged to approximately
4 million. Mass displacement in the 3-10% of population range from the acute
Taliban takeover crisis.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Situation Report.' October 2021.
US Department of Defense. 'Operation Allies Refuge Statistics.' 2021.

2022.assessment:
Significant ongoing outflows. Total external Afghan refugees reached approximately
6.4 million (world's second largest refugee population after Ukraine conflict).
Pakistan approximately 1.4-2 million registered plus significant undocumented.
Iran approximately 3.4 million (registered and undocumented). IDP approximately
3.2 million. Outflows continued driven by economic collapse, rights restrictions,
and persecution. Coerced returns from Pakistan and Iran added complexity.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Global Trends Forced Displacement 2022.' Geneva.

2023.assessment:
Significant outflows continuing. Pakistan announced mass expulsion of undocumented
Afghans (approximately 1.7 million), forcing returns of people who feared Taliban
persecution — refoulement concerns. External refugees approximately 6 million.
IDP approximately 3.2 million. Coerced returns from Pakistan complicated the
picture. Ongoing outflows of people fleeing Taliban rule, especially women.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Pakistan-Afghanistan Returns Crisis 2023.' Geneva.

2024.assessment:
Significant outflows continuing. External Afghan refugee population approximately
6 million. IDP approximately 3.2 million. Pakistan continued expulsions. Iran
conducting forced returns. Women and girls fleeing Taliban gender apartheid laws.
Ongoing significant outflows despite coerced returns complicating total figures.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Situation.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
Significant outflows continued. Afghanistan remained the world's largest or
second-largest displacement situation. External refugees approximately 5.5-6 million.
Coerced returns from Pakistan and Iran continued. Women and minorities disproportionately
displaced by Taliban governance. Ongoing significant outflows from Taliban rule.

sources.citation: UNHCR. 'Afghanistan Displacement.' 2025.

1996.assessment:
No international sanctions regime targeted Afghanistan in 1996. Taliban takeover
of Kabul in September 1996 drew international condemnation but no formal sanctions.
The UN continued recognizing the Rabbani government. No UNSC sanctions resolution
against Afghanistan or the Taliban had been passed.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records. 1996.

1997.assessment:
No formal international sanctions against Afghanistan or Taliban in 1997. US
government imposed domestic restrictions on Taliban but no UNSC sanctions.
Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and UAE recognition of Taliban created diplomatic split
but no sanctions regime.

sources.citation: US Department of State. Background Notes: Afghanistan. 1997.

1998.assessment:
No UNSC sanctions against Afghanistan in 1998. US cruise missile strikes against
Al-Qaeda camps in August 1998 (after embassy bombings) were unilateral military
action, not a sanctions regime. UNSC discussed but had not yet passed sanctions.
US Treasury designated some Al-Qaeda entities under IEEPA.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records. 1998.

1999.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1267 (October 15, 1999) imposed targeted sanctions on the Taliban:
flight ban on Ariana Afghan Airlines, asset freeze on Taliban funds. These were
targeted at the Taliban leadership and Bin Laden/Al-Qaeda, demanding extradition
of Bin Laden. The sanctions targeted specific individuals and entities, not
Afghanistan's economy or population broadly. The Sanctions Committee (1267
Committee) was established to administer the regime.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1267 (1999). S/RES/1267. October 15, 1999.

2000.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1333 (December 19, 2000) significantly expanded Taliban sanctions:
arms embargo, closure of Taliban offices abroad, ban on provision of acetic
anhydride (precursor for heroin processing), additional asset freezes and travel
bans. This moved from targeted individuals to broader sectoral restrictions
including arms embargo affecting Afghanistan's conflict dynamics. Sanctions
remained focused on Taliban as entity rather than Afghan state broadly.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1333 (2000). S/RES/1333. December 19, 2000.

2001.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1390 (January 2002, retroactively applicable) and UNSC 1363
(July 2001) maintained Taliban/Al-Qaeda sanctions. Post-September 11, the 9/11
attacks led to UNSC Resolution 1373 (anti-terrorism) and further asset freezes
of Al-Qaeda/Taliban funds globally. Post-Bonn, the new Afghan Interim Authority
was not sanctioned — sanctions regime split: Taliban/Al-Qaeda individuals
continued on sanctions list while new Afghan state was unsanctioned.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1363 (2001). S/RES/1363. July 30, 2001.
UN Security Council Resolution 1373 (2001). S/RES/1373. September 28, 2001.

2002.assessment:
After Taliban collapse, UNSC Resolution 1390 (January 2002) maintained targeted
sanctions against Taliban and Al-Qaeda individuals on the consolidated list
(travel ban, asset freeze, arms embargo on listed individuals). The Afghan
Interim Authority and later Transitional Authority were not subject to sanctions.
Sanctions were individualized, targeting the Taliban as an insurgent movement
and Al-Qaeda. Afghan state could receive international aid and investment freely.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1390 (2002). S/RES/1390. January 16, 2002.

2003.assessment:
Targeted sanctions continued under 1267 Committee. Taliban and Al-Qaeda
individuals on consolidated list subject to travel ban, asset freeze, arms
embargo. Afghan government fully integrated into international financial
system. Major donor conferences (Tokyo 2002, Berlin 2004) channeled aid to
Afghan state. No sanctions against Afghan state or economy.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1526 (2004). S/RES/1526.

2004.assessment:
Targeted sanctions regime continued. UNSC 1526 (January 2004) renewed and
strengthened targeted measures against Taliban/Al-Qaeda individuals. Afghan
government signed international agreements, accessed World Bank, IMF, Asian
Development Bank financing. No sanctions on Afghan state.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1526 (2004). S/RES/1526. January 30, 2004.

2005.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions regime continued under 1267 Committee. London
Conference on Afghanistan (2006) signaled international engagement. Afghan
state not sanctioned. Taliban commanders and Al-Qaeda leaders on consolidated
sanctions list. Targeted measures: asset freeze, travel ban, arms embargo
on listed individuals only.

sources.citation: UN 1267 Sanctions Committee. Consolidated List 2005.

2006.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1735 (2006) updated 1267 regime. Targeted sanctions on Taliban
and Al-Qaeda individuals continued. Afghan government not sanctioned. London
Compact signed February 2006 between Afghanistan and international community
affirmed major aid commitments. No restrictions on Afghan state economic activity.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1735 (2006). S/RES/1735. December 22, 2006.

2007.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions regime continued. UNSC 1822 (2008) later bifurcated
Taliban and Al-Qaeda lists. In 2007 the consolidated list remained in force with
targeted measures against Taliban commanders. Afghan state had full access to
international financial institutions and aid flows.

sources.citation: UN 1267 Committee. Consolidated Sanctions List 2007.

2008.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1822 (June 2008) split the 1267 consolidated list into separate
Taliban sanctions list and Al-Qaeda sanctions list, recognizing different political
status. Taliban-specific sanctions targeted individuals: travel ban, asset freeze,
arms embargo on listed persons. Afghan government not sanctioned. Paris Conference
June 2008 pledged $21 billion in aid to Afghanistan.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1822 (2008). S/RES/1822. June 30, 2008.

2009.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Taliban continued. Afghan government not
sanctioned. US imposed additional financial sanctions on Taliban-linked
individuals under Treasury OFAC designations. International aid increased
substantially with Obama surge. No sectoral sanctions on Afghanistan.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC. 'Specially Designated Nationals List.' 2009.

2010.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1988 (June 2011, retro to 2010 policy discussions) formally
created separate Taliban Sanctions Committee (1988 Committee) distinct from
Al-Qaeda Committee. Taliban sanctions specifically designed with peace process
flexibility in mind. Targeted individual measures continued: 135 individuals
on Taliban consolidated list. Afghan state not sanctioned.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1988 (2011). S/RES/1988. June 17, 2011.

2011.assessment:
UNSC Resolution 1988 (June 2011) established separate Taliban Sanctions Committee,
explicitly with provisions for peace process flexibility including delisting
procedures. Taliban individual sanctions maintained (travel ban, asset freeze,
arms embargo on listed persons). Afghan state completely unsanctioned. Bonn II
Conference planned for December 2011.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1988 (2011). S/RES/1988. June 17, 2011.

2012.assessment:
Targeted Taliban individual sanctions continued under 1988 Committee. Taliban
political office opened in Doha Qatar, opening questions about sanctions regime.
Afghan state fully engaged in international system. Tokyo Mutual Accountability
Framework 2012 conditioned aid on governance benchmarks but was not sanctions.

sources.citation: UN 1988 Sanctions Committee. Annual Report 2012.

2013.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Taliban under 1988 Committee continued. Five
Taliban prisoners exchanged for Bowe Bergdahl (completed 2014) tested sanctions
regime flexibility. Afghan state fully integrated internationally. No sectoral
sanctions on Afghanistan.

sources.citation: UN 1988 Sanctions Committee. Reports 2013.

2014.assessment:
Targeted sanctions continued. Bergdahl exchange: five Taliban leaders (Khairullah
Khairkhwa, Mullah Fazl, Mullah Norullah Nori, Mohammad Nabi Omari, Abdul Haq
Wasiq) removed from 1988 list under travel exclusion to Qatar for one year.
Afghan state fully engaged internationally. NUG established October 2014.
No sanctions on Afghan state.

sources.citation: UN Security Council. 'Bergdahl Exchange Notification.' 2014.

2015.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Taliban under 1988 Committee. Afghan state not
sanctioned. US continued OFAC Taliban designations. No changes to fundamental
sanctions architecture. Afghan NUG fully engaged internationally. Heart of Asia
Istanbul Process diplomatic engagement continued.

sources.citation: UN 1988 Sanctions Committee. Annual Report 2015.

2016.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions continued under 1988 Committee. Afghan state not
sanctioned. Brussels Conference on Afghanistan October 2016 pledged $15.2 billion
through 2020. The sanctions regime remained focused on individual Taliban
commanders with travel bans, asset freezes, and arms embargo on listed persons.

sources.citation: Brussels Conference on Afghanistan. 'Self-Reliance through Mutual Accountability.' October 2016.

2017.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions on Taliban under 1988 Committee continued.
Afghan state fully integrated in international system. UNSC monitoring reports
noted Taliban leadership approximately 144 individuals on list. No sanctions
on Afghan economy or state institutions.

sources.citation: UN Security Council. 'Report of the Analytical Support and Sanctions Monitoring Team S/2017/409.' 2017.

2018.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions continued. US-Taliban peace talks beginning in
Doha raised questions about sanctions regime flexibility. Taliban representatives
participated in talks without formal sanctions waivers. Afghan state fully
unsanctioned. No changes to fundamental architecture.

sources.citation: UN 1988 Sanctions Monitoring Team. Report 2018.

2019.assessment:
Targeted individual sanctions continued. Doha Agreement negotiations included
discussion of sanctions relief for Taliban. US offered to discuss delisting
as part of peace deal. UNSC 1988 targeted sanctions on approximately 135
Taliban individuals maintained. Afghan state fully engaged internationally.

sources.citation: UN Security Council. 'S/2019/481 Monitoring Team Report.' 2019.

2020.assessment:
Doha Agreement (February 29, 2020) included US commitment to seek UNSC delisting
of Taliban leaders. Some delisting requests filed. Targeted individual sanctions
continued under 1988 regime. Afghan state not sanctioned. UNSC reviewed list with
some delistings to facilitate peace process. Taliban Doha office participants had
de facto sanctions travel exemptions for negotiations.

sources.citation: US-Taliban Doha Agreement. 'Agreement for Bringing Peace to Afghanistan.' February 29, 2020.

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 2021 triggered major new sanctions regime. US froze
approximately $9.5 billion in Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB) assets held at Federal
Reserve Bank of New York under Executive Order. EO 14064 issued February 2022
bifurcated frozen assets. UNSC maintained 1988 Taliban targeted sanctions.
US Treasury issued General License 14 for humanitarian transactions.
The broad economic effect was severe despite nominal humanitarian exemptions —
the frozen DAB assets effectively collapsed the banking system. Multiple
humanitarian exemptions issued (GL14, GL15, GL19, GL20) to allow aid flows.

sources.citation: US Executive Order 14064. February 11, 2022. Blocking Property and Prohibiting Certain Transactions With Respect to Afghanistan.
US Treasury OFAC. General License 14. August 2021.

2022.assessment:
EO 14064 (February 2022) maintained freeze on ~$7 billion of DAB assets (remainder
to Afghan Fund for Reconstruction). Afghan Fund established to hold $3.5 billion
for eventual release. 1988 Committee targeted Taliban sanctions maintained.
UNSC Resolution 2615 (December 2021) humanitarian exemption enshrined. Multiple
US Treasury general licenses expanded humanitarian space. Economic sanctions
effect remained severe — banking system not restored, imports restricted,
aid dependent economy.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 2615 (2021). S/RES/2615. December 22, 2021.
Afghan Fund for Reconstruction. Establishment Documents. September 2022.

2023.assessment:
Broad sanctions and asset freeze regime continued. DAB assets ($7B frozen at Fed)
debate continued. Afghan Fund released $40M in first tranche for humanitarian
procurement. 1988 targeted Taliban sanctions maintained. US Treasury general
licenses expanded to facilitate humanitarian operations. Banking system remained
severely compromised. SWIFT access limited.

sources.citation: US Treasury. 'Afghan Sanctions Policy Update.' 2023.

2024.assessment:
Sanctions architecture unchanged. DAB assets ($7B) remained frozen. Afghan Fund
continued limited humanitarian disbursements. 1988 Taliban targeted sanctions
maintained. US general licenses provided humanitarian space but commercial
banking severely restricted. Taliban EITI delisting June 2024 further reduced
international engagement. No pathway to unfreezing central bank assets under
current political conditions.

sources.citation: UN Analytical Support and Sanctions Monitoring Team. 'Report S/2024/92.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
Sanctions regime continued. DAB assets freeze maintained. Afghan Fund humanitarian
disbursements continued at limited scale. 1988 targeted Taliban sanctions in force.
International community debated conditionality for asset release but Taliban
made no concessions on women's rights or governance. Economic strangulation
continued with humanitarian carve-outs insufficient to reverse economic collapse.

sources.citation: UN OCHA. 'Afghanistan Humanitarian Update.' 2025.

1996.assessment:
Taliban consolidated control over Kabul in 1996 and began imposing extreme restrictions on
civil liberties. Women were banned from education, employment, and public life. Music, television,
and most forms of cultural expression were banned. Freedom of expression, assembly, and religion
were non-existent for those not adhering to the Taliban's interpretation of Islam.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997, Afghanistan

1997.assessment:
Taliban controlled most of Afghanistan by 1997, extending extreme restrictions on civil
liberties. No free press, no political opposition, no freedoms of religion for minorities.
The Northern Alliance controlled approximately 10% of the country, creating a divided state.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998, Afghanistan

1998.assessment:
Taliban continued extreme suppression of all civil liberties in 1998. The Taliban captured
Mazar-i-Sharif and massacred Hazara civilians. Extreme religious police enforced dress codes,
banned music, and controlled all aspects of public life. No freedom of press, expression,
assembly, or religion.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999, Afghanistan

1999.assessment:
Taliban rule continued to enforce total suppression of civil liberties in 1999. UN sanctions
were imposed on the Taliban regime following the 1998 US embassy bombings. All forms of
media and expression remained banned, women continued to be excluded from public life,
and political dissent was impossible.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000, Afghanistan

2000.assessment:
Taliban rule in 2000 maintained the lowest possible civil liberties score. Taliban enforced
the strictest interpretation of Islamic law, banning television, music, kite-flying, and
imposing severe dress codes and movement restrictions on women. The Taliban continued
providing sanctuary to Al-Qaeda.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001, Afghanistan

2001.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at the worst level through most of 2001 under the Taliban.
After the September 11 attacks, the US-led invasion began in October. By late November-December,
the Taliban had been ousted from major cities. The year ends with the Bonn Agreement
establishing an Interim Authority, beginning a transition away from total repression.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002, Afghanistan

2002.assessment:
Civil liberties improved markedly from Taliban-era lows in 2002, though conditions
remained poor. The transitional government allowed return of women to work and education,
reopening of schools, and partial media freedom. However, warlords controlled much of
the country, and security remained threatening to basic freedoms. Freedom House rated
Afghanistan CL 6, "Not Free."

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003, Afghanistan

2003.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL 6 in 2003 as the transitional government and Loya Jirga
process continued. Freedom of expression and media improved in urban areas, particularly
Kabul. However, warlord control of provinces restricted freedoms outside major cities,
and women faced continued discrimination especially in rural areas.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Afghanistan

2004.assessment:
Civil liberties improved to CL 5 in 2004, reflecting the first presidential election
(October 2004), the new constitution adopted in January 2004, and modest improvements
in media freedom and women's rights in urban areas. The country was rated "Partly Free"
for the first time. Significant restrictions continued in conflict-affected areas.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Afghanistan

2005.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained at CL 5 in 2005 after parliamentary elections in September.
A vibrant media environment was developing in Kabul with dozens of newspapers and television
stations. However, insurgency in the south and east restricted freedoms in those areas.
Women continued to face discrimination and violence, particularly in rural areas.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Afghanistan

2006.assessment:
Civil liberties at CL 5 in 2006 as Taliban insurgency intensified in the south. Civil
society improvements in urban areas contrasted with severe restrictions in Taliban-controlled
or contested regions. Freedom of expression improved in Kabul, but journalists faced
increasing threats and some were killed or threatened.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Afghanistan, PR 5, CL 5, Partly Free

2007.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL 5 in 2007 despite significant security deterioration.
The insurgency spread beyond the south to affect many parts of the country. Some legislators
attempted to pass a blasphemy law that would have severely restricted expression. Afghan
journalist killings increased. Urban civil society continued to develop.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Afghanistan, CL 5

2008.assessment:
Civil liberties at CL 5 in 2008. Violence increased significantly with record civilian
casualties. Journalists remained targets of Taliban and government-connected figures.
Women's rights remained contested, with some legislative attempts to restrict them further.
Taliban control of rural areas expanded, restricting freedoms in those zones.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Afghanistan, CL 5

2009.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL 5 in 2009 but conditions in conflict zones deteriorated.
Presidential elections in August were marred by massive fraud. The Shi'a Personal Status
Law passed, severely restricting women's rights in family matters. Taliban influence over
large rural areas continued to restrict freedoms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Afghanistan, PR 6, CL 5

2010.assessment:
Civil liberties deteriorated to CL 6 in 2010 as security conditions worsened across
more of the country. The Kabul Bank scandal revealed pervasive corruption. Parliamentary
elections in September were disputed. Taliban attacks on civilians and civil society
actors increased. Journalists and human rights defenders faced higher threat levels.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Afghanistan, PR 6, CL 6

2011.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL 6 in 2011, the highest-conflict year in the war to date
with over 7,000 conflict deaths. Taliban controlled or contested much of rural Afghanistan,
preventing exercise of civil liberties in those areas. Journalists continued to be killed.
Osama bin Laden's killing in Pakistan raised Taliban retaliatory threats.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Afghanistan, CL 6, Not Free
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Afghanistan
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Afghanistan, CL 6
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Afghanistan
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Afghanistan
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Afghanistan

2017.assessment:
Civil liberties at 14/60 (equivalent to CL 6 on the legacy scale) in 2017. Afghanistan
remained Not Free. Taliban controlled or contested over 40% of districts. Journalists,
civil society, and human rights defenders continued to be targeted. The Taliban's shadow
governance denied civil liberties to populations under its control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Afghanistan, 14/60 CL, Not Free
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Afghanistan, Not Free
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Afghanistan, Not Free

2020.assessment:
Civil liberties CL score approximately 5 in 2020 with overall score of 27/100. Afghanistan
was Not Free but retained some civil society functioning under the Republic government
prior to the Taliban return. The Doha Agreement raised concerns about potential Taliban
return to repression.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Afghanistan, 27/100 Not Free

2021.assessment:
Civil liberties collapsed to the worst level following the Taliban takeover in August 2021.
Freedom House gave Afghanistan overall 10/100, CL 9/60 (effectively CL 7) — "Not Free."
Women were banned from secondary education, work, and public life. Political opposition
was eliminated. Free expression became life-threatening. All elements of civil society
functioning under the Republic government were dismantled.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Afghanistan, 10/100, CL 9/60

2022.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at the worst level in 2022 under Taliban rule. Women were
progressively excluded from all areas of public life with bans on girls' education,
women's employment, and women's movement without male escort. No political freedom,
no free press, no functioning civil society existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Afghanistan

2023.assessment:
Civil liberties at worst level in 2023. Taliban continued tightening restrictions including
banning women from universities. Afghanistan received among the lowest possible scores from
Freedom House, reflecting total absence of political rights and civil liberties under
Taliban theocratic governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Afghanistan

2024.assessment:
Afghanistan received a CL score of 5/60 (worst tier) and overall 6/100 in Freedom
House 2025 report covering 2024. Taliban governance continued to systematically deny all
civil liberties, with further restrictions imposed on women's rights, freedom of expression,
and political dissent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Afghanistan, PR 1/40, CL 5/60, 6/100

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet available. Freedom House 2025 report not yet published. Afghanistan rated Not Free with score ~1/100 under Taliban; women banned from education/work/public life; severe press restrictions; no political freedoms.

1996.assessment:
Taliban captured Kabul in September 1996 and established theocratic rule. The
Northern Alliance (Rabbani, Massoud, Dostum, Ismail Khan) was pushed north but
remained a coherent armed opposition controlling roughly 10% of territory. Taliban
internal cohesion was strong under Mullah Omar but national elite integration was
nonexistent — the old Afghan state elite was displaced or in exile.

sources.citation: Rashid, Ahmed. 'Taliban: Militant Islam, Oil and Fundamentalism in Central Asia.' Yale University Press, 2000.

1997.assessment:
Taliban consolidated control in the south and central Afghanistan. The Northern
Alliance retained a coherent government-in-exile recognized by the UN. Dostum
suffered a mutiny (Malik defection) and lost Mazar-i-Sharif temporarily then
retook it. Significant armed competition between Taliban and Northern Alliance
continued across northern Afghanistan.

sources.citation: Rashid, Ahmed. 'Taliban.' Yale University Press, 2000.

1998.assessment:
Taliban captured Mazar-i-Sharif in August 1998 and massacred thousands of Hazaras,
consolidating control over roughly 90% of Afghanistan. Northern Alliance reduced to
Panjshir Valley and northeast. Taliban governance was centralized under Mullah Omar
with significant Al-Qaeda integration. Elite competition between Taliban and Northern
Alliance continued but Taliban dominated.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. 'Afghanistan: The Massacre in Mazar-i-Sharif.' 1998.

1999.assessment:
Taliban dominated with Northern Alliance confined to small northeastern territory.
Ahmed Shah Massoud remained key opposition commander. Taliban internal cohesion
strong but factional tensions between Pakistani-influenced commanders and traditional
Pashtun commanders occasionally surfaced. UNSC 1267 imposed sanctions targeting
Taliban leadership. International isolation deepened.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1267 (1999). S/RES/1267.

2000.assessment:
Taliban maintained control. Northern Alliance held small territory in northeast.
The Shura-e-Nazar (Massoud's council) received limited Iranian and Russian backing.
Taliban governance consolidated under Mullah Omar. Competition between Taliban and
Northern Alliance remained the defining elite divide. UNSC 1333 tightened sanctions
in December 2000 including arms embargo.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolution 1333 (2000). S/RES/1333.

2001.assessment:
September 11 attacks led to US-led invasion in October 2001. Taliban regime
collapsed within weeks as US airpower combined with Northern Alliance ground
forces. Taliban leadership fled to Pakistan. The Bonn Agreement in December 2001
established interim authority under Hamid Karzai. Complete fragmentation of
Taliban governing structure; new factional competition among Northern Alliance
commanders, warlords, and Pashtun leaders began immediately.

sources.citation: Bonn Agreement. 'Agreement on Provisional Arrangements in Afghanistan.' December 5, 2001.

2002.assessment:
Interim Authority under Karzai established but weak. Northern Alliance warlords
(Dostum, Ismail Khan, Fahim, Qasim Fahim as Defense Minister) controlled their
regional fiefdoms. The Emergency Loya Jirga in June 2002 established Transitional
Authority. Serious warlord competition and factional conflict characterized elite
relations. Karzai relied on US backing to balance competing factions rather than
institutional authority.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan: The Problem of Pashtun Alienation.' Asia Report No. 62, 2003.

2003.assessment:
Open factional conflict among warlords continued. Ismail Khan dominated Herat.
Dostum controlled northern Afghanistan. Fahim and Tajiks dominated security
ministries. Constitutional Loya Jirga convened December 2003. Karzai managed
competing factions through patronage and US support but exercised limited
independent authority. Taliban insurgency began reasserting itself in southern
Afghanistan.

sources.citation: Rubin, Barnett. 'Crafting a Constitution for Afghanistan.' Journal of Democracy 15(3), 2004.

2004.assessment:
October 2004 presidential election won by Karzai with 55% of vote. Constitution
established. Karzai began managing warlords more effectively — Ismail Khan was
moved to cabinet in 2004, reducing his power in Herat. Elite competition remained
but was increasingly managed through state institutions and patronage networks.
Abdullah Abdullah ran against Karzai. Northern Alliance leaders integrated into
government structures.

sources.citation: OSCE/ODIHR. 'Afghanistan Presidential Election 2004 Final Report.' 2005.

2005.assessment:
Parliamentary elections held in September 2005. Karzai continued managing warlord
elite through patronage and appointments. Dostum remained influential in the north.
The Wolesi Jirga included many warlords and strongmen, integrating them into the
formal political system. Competition was significant but managed through electoral
and patronage channels. Taliban insurgency growing in south.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan's New Legislature: Making Democracy Work.' Asia Report No. 116, 2006.

2006.assessment:
Karzai government maintained managed elite competition. NATO ISAF expansion
increased international backing. Warlord patronage networks continued. Taliban
insurgency intensified in Helmand and Kandahar. Elite cohesion at national level
was fragile but managed; regional commanders retained autonomous power. Karzai
balanced Tajik, Uzbek, Hazara, and Pashtun factions.

sources.citation: Giustozzi, Antonio. 'Koran, Kalashnikov and Laptop: The Neo-Taliban Insurgency in Afghanistan.' Columbia University Press, 2008.

2007.assessment:
Elite competition managed through patronage. Karzai relied on warlords and
strongmen as power brokers. Ahmad Wali Karzai (brother) was key power broker
in Kandahar. Northern powerbrokers maintained regional autonomy. Security
deterioration added pressure on elite cohesion. Taliban insurgency expanded
to previously stable provinces.

sources.citation: Waldman, Matt. 'Falling Short: Aid Effectiveness in Afghanistan.' ACBAR, 2008.

2008.assessment:
Managed elite competition continued. Karzai balancing act under increasing stress
from security deterioration. Parliamentary and presidential election preparations
began. Northern Alliance figures contested power through cabinet positions.
International criticism of Karzai's governance growing. Elite cohesion remained
fragile but functional at national level.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan: The Need for International Resolve.' Asia Report No. 145, 2008.

2009.assessment:
The August 2009 presidential election was severely contested and fraudulent.
Karzai won officially but Independent Election Commission found widespread fraud.
Abdullah Abdullah withdrew before the second round citing refusal of fraud
investigation. This represented open elite conflict: the electoral process
fractured elite legitimacy. US pushed for electoral reform. Elite cohesion
at breaking point during election crisis.

sources.citation: ECC (Electoral Complaints Commission). 'Final Report on 2009 Presidential Election.' 2009.
Suhrke, Astri. 'Democratizing a Dependent State: The Case of Afghanistan.' Democratization 18(3), 2011.

2010.assessment:
Post-election crisis stabilized. Karzai retained presidency. Parliamentary
elections in September 2010 saw continued warlord competition. IEC invalidated
25% of votes initially. Karzai managed elite competition through patronage
appointments. The Wolesi Jirga crisis extended into 2011 over election results.
Elite competition managed but contentious.

sources.citation: FEFA (Free and Fair Election Foundation of Afghanistan). '2010 Parliamentary Elections Report.' 2011.

2011.assessment:
Karzai government managed elite competition. Ahmad Wali Karzai assassinated
July 2011, destabilizing patronage networks in Kandahar. US-Afghan relations
strained over security agreement negotiations. Elite competition remained
managed but fraught. NATO drawdown planning added uncertainty to power
calculations among elites.

sources.citation: ICG. 'The Insurgency in Afghanistan's Heartland.' Asia Report No. 207, 2011.

2012.assessment:
Elite competition managed through patronage. Karzai preparing for constitutional
succession (term-limited for 2014). Elite maneuvering for post-Karzai era began.
Northern Alliance figures (Abdullah, Fahim) positioning. Transition planning
with NATO dominated political space. Significant competition managed through
patronage and international mediation.

sources.citation: Integrity Watch Afghanistan. 'Afghan Perceptions and Experiences of Corruption.' 2012.

2013.assessment:
Pre-election elite competition intensified as 2014 presidential race approached.
Multiple candidates emerged including Abdullah Abdullah, Ashraf Ghani, Zalmai
Rassoul. Karzai's second term ending. Elite jockeying through candidate alliances
and ethnic bargaining. Competition was significant but managed within electoral
framework.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan's Parties in Transition.' Asia Briefing No. 141, 2014.

2014.assessment:
The 2014 presidential election produced open elite conflict. Abdullah Abdullah
led first round, Ashraf Ghani won second round amid massive fraud allegations.
Both candidates declared victory. The crisis lasted months requiring US Secretary
Kerry mediation. The resulting National Unity Government (NUG) with Ghani as
President and Abdullah as CEO was a power-sharing pact under international
pressure, not a legitimate electoral outcome. Structural open elite conflict
embedded in the governing arrangement.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan's Secular Parties Look to 2014.' Asia Briefing No. 132, 2013.
Ruttig, Thomas. 'The 2014 Presidential Elections.' Afghanistan Analysts Network, 2014.

2015.assessment:
NUG dysfunctional from inception. Ghani and Abdullah competed over cabinet
appointments, parallel bureaucracies, and policy. The CEO position (held by
Abdullah) was constitutionally undefined. Northern Alliance constituencies
aligned with Abdullah, Pashtun factions with Ghani. Open factional conflict
was institutionalized within the governing structure. Mullah Omar's death
revealed July 2015 complicated Taliban side.

sources.citation: Mukhopadhyay, Dipali. 'Afghanistan's National Unity Government at One Year.' USIP, 2015.

2016.assessment:
NUG continued with persistent Ghani-Abdullah tensions. Parliamentary election
delays and IEC paralysis added dysfunction. Warlords (Dostum as VP, Ismail Khan)
operated semi-independently. The Quadrilateral Coordination Group (QCG) peace
process with Pakistan dominated diplomatic space but elite conflict continued
domestically. Taliban captured Kunduz briefly in 2015, Lashkar Gah threatened.

sources.citation: SIGAR. 'Quarterly Report to the United States Congress.' July 2016.

2017.assessment:
Ghani-Abdullah NUG tensions continued. Dostum fled to Turkey after rape accusation
against political rival, removing key powerbroker. Anti-Ghani political movement
formed (Dostum, Atta Noor, Mohaqiq). Parliamentary elections repeatedly delayed.
Open factional conflict between NUG partners and within Ghani's coalition.
Trump's South Asia strategy announced in August 2017.

sources.citation: Ruttig, Thomas. 'The New Anti-Government Alliance.' Afghanistan Analysts Network, 2017.

2018.assessment:
Parliamentary elections finally held October 2018 after three-year delay amid
serious technical failures (biometric system failures, IEC credibility issues).
NUG dysfunction continued. Ghani-Abdullah rivalry structural. US-Taliban talks
began in Doha. Warlord competition (Atta Noor dismissed as Balkh governor,
refused to leave) represented open challenge to central authority.

sources.citation: FEFA. '2018 Parliamentary Elections Report.' 2019.

2019.assessment:
September 2019 presidential election again disputed. Results delayed months.
Ghani declared winner February 2020, Abdullah contested and claimed independent
victory. US-Taliban Doha Agreement signed February 2020 without Kabul government
involvement, demonstrating NUG's marginalization. Elite conflict between Ghani
and Abdullah reached peak ahead of dual inauguration crisis.

sources.citation: IEC. '2019 Presidential Election Final Results.' February 2020.

2020.assessment:
March 2020 dual inauguration: both Ghani and Abdullah held separate presidential
inauguration ceremonies on the same day, each declaring themselves president.
This represented the most acute elite fragmentation of the republic period.
Kerry-type mediation required again; power-sharing deal reached in May 2020
with Abdullah heading High Council for National Reconciliation. Active power
struggles at peak intensity. US withdrawal plans and Taliban talks added
existential pressure on elite cohesion.

sources.citation: Ruttig, Thomas. 'The Dual Inauguration.' Afghanistan Analysts Network, March 2020.
US Department of State. 'Political Agreement Between Ghani and Abdullah.' May 17, 2020.

2021.assessment:
Republic-side elite completely fragmented as Taliban advanced May-August 2021.
Ghani fled Kabul August 15 as Taliban entered without resistance. Provincial
governors and military commanders surrendered or fled. Republic elite dissolved.
Taliban then established Taliban Islamic Emirate with rapid consolidation:
Haibatullah Akhundzada as Supreme Leader, Mullah Baradar sidelined,
Haqqani network in Interior Ministry. Taliban governance unified under
supreme religious leadership. By end of 2021 Taliban governance was
consolidated under stable unified structure despite internal Baradar/Haqqani tensions.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan's Taliban: Victorious but Divided.' Asia Report No. 312, 2021.
Giustozzi, Antonio. 'The Taliban at War 2001-2018.' Hurst Publishers, 2019.

2022.assessment:
Taliban governance consolidated. Haibatullah Akhundzada ruling from Kandahar
as Supreme Leader. Sirajuddin Haqqani as Interior Minister. Acting PM Mullah
Hassan Akhund. Internal Taliban debates over governance (Baradar marginalized)
resolved in favor of hardline Kandahar-based leadership. No meaningful elite
competition from outside Taliban structure. Opposition in exile fragmented
and ineffective.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Taliban Governance and the International Community.' Asia Report No. 322, 2022.

2023.assessment:
Taliban governance continued consolidated. National Resistance Front (Massoud)
conducted limited operations in Panjshir but represented no serious threat.
Taliban Supreme Leadership Council maintained cohesion. Haibatullah rarely
appeared publicly. Governance disputes over policy (women's education, international
engagement) remained internal to Taliban Shura. No significant elite competition
from outside the movement.

sources.citation: UN Analytical Support and Sanctions Monitoring Team. 'Report S/2023/370.' 2023.

2024.assessment:
Taliban governance remained consolidated under Supreme Leader Haibatullah.
Extreme restrictions on women codified. International isolation continued.
Taliban internal cohesion maintained despite governance challenges including
economic collapse and international sanctions. No meaningful internal elite
competition publicly visible. IS-K represented security threat but not elite
competition within Taliban structure.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Afghanistan. 'Report A/HRC/55/80.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
Taliban governance continued consolidated into 2025. Afghanistan's Compact
diplomacy resumed in Doha. Taliban maintained unified leadership structure.
Haibatullah's authority unchallenged within the movement. Opposition in exile
remained fragmented. No signs of significant internal elite fracture despite
economic deterioration and humanitarian crisis.

sources.citation: UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA). 'Annual Report 2024.' 2025.

1996.assessment:
Taliban rule replaced the collapsed mujahedeen government with a theocratic system that
had no legislature, no elections, no independent judiciary, and no formal bureaucracy
beyond religious enforcement. All state functions were performed through informal Islamic
councils and the Office of the Supreme Leader. No modern state institutions existed or
functioned in any recognizable form.

sources.citation: ICG; Freedom House 1997 on Afghanistan
Freedom House 1998
Freedom House 1999
Freedom House 2000
Freedom House 2001

2001.assessment:
Institutional collapse in 2001 as Taliban rule ended violently. The Bonn Agreement in
December 2001 established an Interim Authority headed by Hamid Karzai, beginning the
creation of new institutions from scratch. No functioning state institutions existed
at the start of the year; by December, a minimal interim government was established.

sources.citation: UN-supported Bonn Agreement, December 2001; Freedom House 2002

2002.assessment:
Emergency Loya Jirga held in June 2002 established transitional government under Karzai.
Nascent institutions were being created: a new Afghan National Army began forming,
a new police force, and early judicial systems. However, institutions were extremely
weak, fragmented, and dependent on international support. Warlords effectively controlled
much of the country, undermining central institutional authority.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: Judicial Reform and Transitional Justice, January 2003

2003.assessment:
Constitutional Loya Jirga convened in late 2003 to draft and adopt a new constitution.
The new Afghan constitution was adopted in January 2004. Institutions remained embryonic:
the judiciary was non-functional in most provinces, the legislature did not yet exist,
and the bureaucracy was understaffed and corrupt. International advisors filled critical
government functions.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: The Constitutional Loya Jirga, December 2003

2004.assessment:
Institutions began developing in 2004 with the first presidential election in October.
A new constitution was in force. The Afghan National Army was forming and the civil
service was being reconstructed. However, institutions remained severely fragile:
judiciary largely dysfunctional, parliament not yet elected, and bureaucracy dependent
on international aid and advisors. Freedom House PR improved to 5.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Afghanistan, PR 5

2005.assessment:
Parliamentary elections held in September 2005, establishing the Wolesi Jirga. The
legislature was functioning nominally, the executive was elected, and a constitutional
framework existed. However, institutions were still very weak: courts were corrupt and
ineffective, the parliament included warlords and drug lords, and civil service capacity
was minimal.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan's New Legislature: Making Democracy Work, May 2006

2006.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained severely degraded in 2006. The parliament was
operating but frequently gridlocked. The judiciary was corrupt and ineffective in
delivering justice. The Afghan National Army was growing but had limited operational
capacity. Security institutions were undermined by corruption and infiltration.

sources.citation: ICG; Freedom House 2007, Afghanistan

2007.assessment:
Institutional functioning continued at severely degraded levels in 2007. The parliament
was increasingly dysfunctional. Some legislators attempted to pass a blasphemy law
restricting free expression. Corruption within government reached endemic levels.
The Ministry of Interior was deeply infiltrated by criminal networks.

sources.citation: ICG, Confronting Afghanistan's Security Dilemma, October 2007

2008.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained severely degraded in 2008. A Transparency International
report ranked Afghanistan as one of the world's most corrupt countries. Judicial
institutions were nearly non-functional for ordinary Afghans. Parliament was operational
but weak. The army was growing but still dependent on NATO support.

sources.citation: Transparency International 2008; ICG; Freedom House 2009

2009.assessment:
Institutional credibility collapsed in 2009 with the massively fraudulent presidential
election. Karzai was re-elected after a first round result tainted by widespread
fraud; the runoff was cancelled. Freedom House downgraded Afghanistan's political
rights from 5 to 6. The election fraud severely undermined the legitimacy of
electoral institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2010, Afghanistan, PR downgraded from 5 to 6; UN Electoral Mission

2010.assessment:
Institutional functioning continued its decline in 2010. Parliamentary elections were
held in September but were marred by fraud and a political crisis over the results.
The Kabul Bank scandal exposed systemic corruption at the heart of the financial
system. Both political rights and civil liberties were at CL/PR 6 (Not Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House 2011, Afghanistan, PR 6, CL 6

2011.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained severely degraded in 2011. The International Security
Assistance Force transition began, putting increasing pressure on weak Afghan institutions.
Anti-corruption efforts remained ineffective. The judiciary continued to fail ordinary
citizens. Constitutional governance was nominal rather than substantive.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2012; ICG Afghanistan reporting 2011
Freedom House 2013; ICG
Freedom House 2014; SIGAR

2014.assessment:
2014 presidential elections were deeply contested, with both main candidates claiming
victory amid widespread fraud allegations. A US-brokered National Unity Government
was formed months after the election. Institutional legitimacy was severely damaged.
NATO combat mission ended; Afghan institutions expected to function independently.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan's National Unity Government, March 2015
ICG; Freedom House 2016; SIGAR
Freedom House 2017; ICG
Freedom House 2018, Afghanistan, 26/100
Freedom House 2019; ICG
Freedom House 2020; ICG

2020.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained severely degraded in 2020. The 2019 presidential
elections were again contested, with results not certified until February 2020. A
dual government was briefly established (both Ghani and Abdullah claimed the presidency).
Freedom House gave Afghanistan 27/100. Doha Agreement raised the prospect of Taliban
return threatening institutional progress.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2021, Afghanistan, 27/100

2021.assessment:
Complete institutional collapse occurred in August 2021 with the Taliban takeover. All
elected institutions — parliament, presidential office, electoral bodies — were dissolved.
Courts were replaced by Taliban Islamic courts. The civil service was dismantled. All
formal state institutions ceased functioning in any democratic or constitutional sense.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2022, Afghanistan, 10/100; ICG; multiple news sources

2022.assessment:
Taliban established a new governance system in 2022 based on their interpretation of
Islamic law, with no elections, no legislature, no independent judiciary, and no
functioning civil service for most Afghans. Women excluded from governance entirely.
No functioning democratic or legal institutions existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2023, Afghanistan, Not Free; UN reports
Freedom House 2024; UN reports on Afghanistan
Freedom House 2025, Afghanistan, PR 1/40, CL 5/60, 6/100

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet available. V-Dem 2025 data not published. Taliban-run institutions: judiciary applying strict Sharia; parliament dissolved; elections abolished; women excluded from public life. Extremely low liberal democracy score expected.

2004.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure was approximately 2.4% of GDP in 2004. This figure captures only the nascent Afghan National Army and police budgets, not the enormous US-led coalition military presence which was the dominant security force. Presidential elections were held in October 2004.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2004

2005.assessment: Afghan government military spending was approximately 2.0% of GDP. The ratio declined slightly as GDP grew faster than the domestic military budget. Parliamentary elections held in 2005 reinforced constitutional governance, but security remained dependent on international forces.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2005

2006.assessment: Afghan government military spending was approximately 1.9% of GDP. Taliban insurgency intensified in 2006, but the formal Afghan security budget remained modest relative to GDP as US/NATO bore the primary security burden.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2006

2007.assessment: Afghan government military spending rose to 2.6% of GDP as the insurgency intensified and the Afghan National Army expanded. Absolute spending increased 67% from 2006 as NATO surged forces and the Afghan security forces grew.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2007

2008.assessment: Afghan government military spending was 2.3% of GDP. US President Obama's election signaled an imminent surge; Taliban continued expanding operations across the south and east. The Afghan government's own security expenditure remained a fraction of the true security cost.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2008

2009.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure at 2.1% of GDP. The US Obama surge saw 30,000 additional troops deployed; total international military presence reached ~140,000. Afghan security forces were rapidly expanded under ISAF training programs.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2009

2010.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure at 1.9% of GDP. Despite the surge in total international security spending, the formal Afghan government military budget remained modest. GDP growth diluted the ratio even as absolute spending rose.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2010

2011.assessment: Afghan government military spending at 1.8% of GDP. US began drawdown timeline; NATO transition to Afghan lead security began. Osama bin Laden killed in Pakistan in May 2011.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2011

2012.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure fell to 1.2% of GDP as US began drawing down troops. Absolute spending declined from 2011 peak. The Afghan government was formally assigned lead security responsibility in transition provinces.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2012

2013.assessment: Afghan government military spending at 1.1% of GDP, continuing to decline. Afghan forces officially took over full security responsibility across the country in June 2013, though heavily reliant on US/NATO funding and logistical support.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2013

2014.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure rose to 1.3% of GDP as NATO combat mission ended in December 2014. The Ghani-Abdullah National Unity Government formed after disputed elections; security demands increased as Taliban gained ground.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2014

2015.assessment: Afghan government military spending dropped below 1% of GDP in 2015 as fiscal constraints tightened. The Taliban captured Kunduz briefly in September-October 2015, the first provincial capital taken since 2001, demonstrating the deteriorating security situation despite the military spending data.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2015

2016.assessment: Afghan government military spending remained below 1% of GDP. Taliban launched major offensives across multiple provinces. The formal Afghan military budget severely understated the security burden, as US-funded ANA Trust Fund and bilateral assistance provided the bulk of ANA operational funding.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2016

2017.assessment: Afghan government military spending at 0.95% of GDP. US announced new South Asia strategy in August 2017; about 3,000 additional US troops deployed. Taliban controlled or contested around 40% of Afghan territory by year end.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2017

2018.assessment: Afghan government military spending marginally crossed 1% of GDP. US-Taliban peace talks began in Qatar. Civilian casualties reached record highs according to UNAMA. Government forces suffered severe casualties.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2018

2019.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure at 1.2% of GDP. US-Taliban deal nearly finalized then briefly collapsed in September 2019. Presidential elections marred by fraud allegations. Security conditions continued to deteriorate with Taliban controlling more territory.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2019

2020.assessment: Afghan government military spending rose to 1.4% of GDP in 2020. The US-Taliban Doha Agreement was signed in February 2020 committing US forces to withdrawal. Intra-Afghan negotiations stalled. Taliban attacks on Afghan security forces continued despite a brief reduction in violence.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2020

2021.assessment: Afghan government military expenditure appears at 1.8% of GDP for 2021, but this captures only the January-August period before Taliban takeover in August. The collapse of the Afghan National Defense and Security Forces was swift and total; the figure reflects spending by a government that ceased to exist.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS, Afghanistan, 2021

2022.assessment: No data available. Taliban Islamic Emirate does not publish formal national accounts or defense budgets. International sanctions and aid suspension mean no standard reporting mechanisms exist.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS; no data for post-2021 Taliban period
