1996.assessment:
The Afghan civil war continued in 1996 as the Taliban captured Kabul in September.
Fierce battles occurred in and around Kabul as the Taliban defeated the mujahedeen
factions. Violence was pervasive throughout the country with the Taliban-vs-Northern
Alliance conflict. Thousands of combatants and civilians were killed in fighting.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset, Afghanistan conflict, 1996

1997.assessment:
Taliban-vs-Northern Alliance fighting continued in 1997. Taliban captured Mazar-i-Sharif
briefly before being repelled; hundreds were killed in the fighting. Significant battle
deaths occurred throughout the northern and central regions. Violence was intense though
diffuse across multiple fronts.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Dataset, Afghanistan 1997

1998.assessment:
High levels of political violence in 1998. Taliban captured Mazar-i-Sharif in August and
massacred thousands of Hazara civilians. Continued fighting with Northern Alliance. Taliban
also launched US embassy bombing retaliatory threats. Civil war casualties continued at
high levels. Mass atrocities against Hazara minority occurred.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, The Massacre in Mazar-i-Sharif, November 1998

1999.assessment:
Civil war continued with Taliban-vs-Northern Alliance conflict. Violence was ongoing
but at somewhat reduced levels compared to peak fighting years. Northern Alliance retained
control of northeast Afghanistan. UN sanctions imposed. Drought added to civilian suffering.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Dataset, Afghanistan 1999

2000.assessment:
Taliban-vs-Northern Alliance conflict continued in 2000 at moderate levels. Taliban
controlled approximately 90% of the country; Northern Alliance held the northeast.
Violence was ongoing with periodic major offensives. Severe drought exacerbated civilian
suffering. Al-Qaeda training camps operated openly.

sources.citation: UCDP Conflict Dataset, Afghanistan 2000

2001.assessment:
Catastrophic political violence in 2001. The US-led Operation Enduring Freedom began in
October, with intensive bombing of Taliban positions. The Taliban collapsed rapidly,
losing all major cities by December. Thousands of Taliban fighters and civilians were
killed in the air campaign and subsequent ground fighting. The year saw both the end of
the civil war and the beginning of a new international conflict.

sources.citation: Costs of War Project, Brown University, Afghanistan casualties, 2021

2002.assessment:
Political violence dropped significantly in 2002 after the Taliban's collapse. Approximately
400 civilian deaths were recorded, plus smaller numbers of combatant deaths. Some Taliban
fighters regrouped in Pakistan and conducted limited attacks. The Operation Anaconda in
March 2002 was the largest US military operation of the year. Overall violence was at
its lowest since the start of the conflict.

sources.citation: Watson Institute Costs of War Project, Afghanistan, 2002

2003.assessment:
Violence remained relatively low in 2003 with approximately 450 civilian deaths, though
insurgent attacks were beginning to increase. Taliban regrouped in Pakistan's tribal areas
and began conducting increasingly effective attacks, particularly in southern Afghanistan.
The transitional government and international forces were establishing control but insurgency
was embryonic.

sources.citation: Watson Institute Costs of War Project, Afghanistan, 2003

2004.assessment:
Civilian deaths dropped to approximately 230 in 2004, the lowest year on record for the
post-invasion period. The presidential election in October occurred with relatively limited
major violence incidents. Insurgency was rebuilding but had not yet reached the scale of
later years. This was a relative 'window' in the conflict trajectory.

sources.citation: Watson Institute Costs of War Project, Afghanistan, 2004

2005.assessment:
Violence escalated dramatically in 2005, with approximately 1,700 total deaths (including
civilians, insurgents, and security forces) per an Associated Press count. The Taliban
significantly expanded operations with suicide bombings and IED attacks becoming common.
Parliamentary elections were held in September amid increased attacks. Insurgency was
rapidly escalating toward full-scale insurgent war.

sources.citation: Associated Press count reported in multiple sources; cited in Costs of War Project, 2005

2006.assessment:
Violence continued to escalate in 2006 with 929 civilian deaths and a fourfold increase
in insurgent attacks since 2002. NATO took command of ISAF forces and expanded from
the south to all of Afghanistan. The Taliban launched 'Operation Mountain Thrust,' one
of the largest battles of the war. Suicide bombers killed hundreds of civilians in
crowded markets.

sources.citation: Watson Institute Costs of War Project, Afghanistan, 2006; UNAMA figures

2007.assessment:
Civilian deaths surpassed 1,000 for the first time in 2007 at 1,582, with total conflict
deaths much higher. UCDP recorded over 7,000 battle-related deaths in 2011; 2007 was
a major escalation year. Suicide attacks peaked, IED use expanded, and Taliban began
challenging government control in major southern cities. The country was described as
in 'serious danger of falling into Taliban control.'

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, 2007; Watson Institute

2008.assessment:
Civilian casualties reached 2,118 in 2008, a record high at that point. Total conflict
deaths were far higher including thousands of combatants. Over 1,100 Afghan police and
530 Afghan soldiers were killed. NATO estimated 5,000 militants killed. The insurgency
spread beyond the south to affect the east, west, and north. This was the deadliest
year of the war since 2001.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2008

2009.assessment:
Violence continued at very high levels in 2009 with civilian casualties exceeding 2008
levels. The US surge was announced in December. Presidential elections in August were
marred by massive fraud and Taliban attacks. Civilian casualties were consistently above
2,000 per UNAMA. Total conflict deaths remained in the high thousands.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2009

2010.assessment:
Extremely high levels of political violence in 2010 as the US surge peaked. UNAMA reported
civilian casualties rising. UCDP noted Afghanistan had the highest death toll of any
conflict globally for two consecutive years (2010-2011). The Marjah offensive and
Operation Moshtarak were major counterinsurgency operations.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2010

2011.assessment:
2011 was the deadliest year of the Afghanistan war with over 7,000 total conflict deaths
(UCDP), including 3,021 civilian deaths (UNAMA record high at that time). Afghanistan had
the highest battle deaths globally in 2011 per UCDP. The surge was still at peak levels.
Osama bin Laden was killed in Pakistan. The Transition to Afghan security forces began.

sources.citation: UCDP, Organized Violence 1989-2011 Journal of Peace Research; UNAMA civilian casualties 2011

2012.assessment:
Violence remained extremely high in 2012 as surge troops began withdrawing. Civilian
casualties remained above 2,000 per UNAMA. The drawdown began but Taliban maintained
high tempo of attacks. UNAMA recorded another year with over 2,000 civilian deaths.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2012

2013.assessment:
High levels of political violence continued in 2013. The security transition accelerated
with Afghan forces taking the lead. Civilian casualties remained above 2,000. Taliban
attacks continued across multiple provinces as international presence declined.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2013

2014.assessment:
Very high violence in 2014 as NATO combat operations ended formally. UNAMA reported
over 3,000 civilian casualties for the first time. The disputed presidential election
and drawn-out political crisis added to instability. Taliban intensified attacks in
the absence of full NATO combat support.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2014

2015.assessment:
Extreme violence in 2015 as the Taliban captured Kunduz — the first provincial capital
since 2001. UNAMA recorded the highest civilian casualties to that date. Taliban controlled
or contested approximately 30% of districts. IS-K emerged as a new actor in eastern
Afghanistan.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2015

2016.assessment:
High political violence continued in 2016. UNAMA reported record civilian casualties
for the year. Taliban captured Kunduz again briefly in October. IS-K continued attacks
in Nangarhar. Multiple mass casualty attacks occurred in Kabul and other cities.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2016

2017.assessment:
Very high political violence in 2017. UNAMA recorded over 10,000 civilian casualties.
Taliban controlled or contested over 40% of districts. Multiple high-profile attacks
including the massive Zubair Market truck bomb in Kabul (May 2017, 150+ killed). IS-K
also staged major attacks.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2017

2018.assessment:
Very high violence in 2018 with another record civilian casualty year. UNAMA reported
over 10,000 civilian casualties for the third consecutive year. Multiple mass casualty
attacks in Kabul. US-Taliban peace talks began. Taliban controlled or contested over
half of districts by year end.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2018

2019.assessment:
High violence continued in 2019 with US-Taliban peace talks ongoing. Violence was used
by the Taliban as leverage in negotiations. Multiple mass casualty attacks occurred.
Presidential elections were held amid attacks. UNAMA reported another year with over
10,000 civilian casualties.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2019

2020.assessment:
Violence declined somewhat in 2020 after the Doha Agreement was signed in February, but
remained very high. Taliban reduced attacks on US/NATO forces but increased attacks on
Afghan government forces. Intra-Afghan negotiations stalled. Targeted killings of journalists
and civil society activists increased significantly.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2020

2021.assessment:
Political violence was extreme in the first half of 2021 as the Taliban launched a massive
offensive coinciding with the US withdrawal announcement. Taliban captured virtually all
of Afghanistan in a matter of weeks in August 2021. The fall of Kabul ended the active
conflict between Taliban and government forces. Violence from IS-K attacks on Taliban
and civilians continued after the Taliban takeover.

sources.citation: UNAMA, Annual Report on Protection of Civilians in Armed Conflict, Afghanistan 2021; ACLED data

2022.assessment:
Under Taliban rule in 2022, IS-K continued conducting mass casualty attacks including
major bombings of mosques and educational facilities. Taliban conducted counterterrorism
operations against IS-K. Overall violence levels declined compared to the war period
but IS-K attacks kept violence significant.

sources.citation: ACLED, 10 Conflicts to Worry About 2022: Afghanistan

2023.assessment:
Violence in 2023 was primarily from IS-K attacks on Taliban and civilians. The Taliban
conducted counterterrorism operations. Cross-border attacks with Pakistan increased.
Overall conflict intensity was lower than during the 2001-2021 war but IS-K remained
an active threat.

sources.citation: ACLED data, Afghanistan 2023

2024.assessment:
Violence in 2024 continued at reduced levels compared to the 2001-2021 war, primarily
from IS-K attacks. Afghanistan-Pakistan border tensions increased. Taliban launched
attacks into Pakistan in early 2026. IS-K bombings continued to cause civilian casualties.

sources.citation: ACLED data, Afghanistan 2024; Al Jazeera reporting

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet available. ISKP (ISIS-K) attacks continue in 2025. Taliban conducting counter-insurgency operations. Violence lower than pre-2021 civil war levels but persistent.

1996.assessment:
The Taliban captured Kabul in September 1996 and rapidly extended control over much of
Afghanistan. By year end, the Taliban controlled approximately 65-70% of the country,
including all major cities except those in the Panjshir valley and northeast. The Northern
Alliance (United Islamic Front) controlled northeastern Afghanistan. No single authority
controlled the entire country.

sources.citation: ICG reports on Afghanistan, 1996; academic literature

1997.assessment:
Taliban controlled approximately 75-80% of Afghanistan by 1997, having consolidated gains
from the previous year. The Northern Alliance retained control of northeastern Afghanistan
including Panjshir, Takhar, and Badakhshan provinces. Taliban-vs-Northern Alliance front
lines were relatively stable through much of the year.

sources.citation: ICG; academic literature on Afghan conflict 1997

1998.assessment:
Taliban captured Mazar-i-Sharif in August 1998, gaining control of nearly all of
Afghanistan. Taliban controlled approximately 85-90% of the country. Northern Alliance
was reduced to the Panjshir valley and surrounding northeast areas. Taliban rule was
near-total across the country's population centers.

sources.citation: ICG; academic literature on Afghan conflict 1998; HRW reports

1999.assessment:
Taliban maintained approximately 85-90% territorial control in 1999. Northern Alliance
held the northeast. Taliban-vs-Northern Alliance front lines were relatively static.
The Taliban's control over most of the country was firm but the Northern Alliance
remained a resistance force in its redoubt.

sources.citation: SIPRI; ICG reports on Afghanistan, 1999

2000.assessment:
Taliban controlled approximately 90% of Afghanistan by 2000. Northern Alliance was
significantly weakened after losing Taloqan (its de facto capital outside Panjshir) in
September 2000. Taliban rule was essentially complete over most of the country. The
international community refused to recognize Taliban control.

sources.citation: ICG; academic literature on Afghan conflict 2000

2001.assessment:
Dramatic territorial transformation in 2001. Taliban controlled ~90% until the US invasion
began in October. By December, Taliban had lost control of all major cities, retreating
to rural strongholds in the south. Northern Alliance forces (supported by US airpower)
swept across Afghanistan. By year end, the Bonn Agreement interim government was
established in Kabul — a failed state transitioning to international-backed reconstruction.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: Regrouping amid Chaos, 2001

2002.assessment:
The Karzai government controlled Kabul and major cities but exercised limited authority
in rural areas. Warlords controlled most of the provinces outside Kabul. The central
government's writ extended to roughly 30-40% of the country effectively. US/ISAF forces
were present but had a limited footprint outside Kabul. Taliban had regrouped in Pakistan.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: Establishing the Rule of Law, February 2003

2003.assessment:
The transitional government began expanding its reach with the support of ISAF and US
forces, but authority remained limited outside Kabul and major cities. Warlords continued
to dominate the provinces. The Taliban began insurgency operations in the south from
Pakistani bases. Government controlled roughly 40% of territory effectively.

sources.citation: ICG, Disarmament and Reintegration in Afghanistan, September 2003

2004.assessment:
Government control expanded modestly in 2004 with presidential elections demonstrating
a degree of state reach. A new constitution was adopted. However, warlords remained
influential in the provinces and Taliban insurgency was growing in the south. Government
held most urban centers but rural southern areas were increasingly contested.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: From Presidential to Parliamentary Elections, 2004

2005.assessment:
Government control was increasingly challenged in southern Afghanistan as Taliban
insurgency expanded. Parliamentary elections held. Northern and central provinces were
relatively stable under government control, but Helmand, Kandahar, Zabul, and Uruzgan
saw growing Taliban presence. Government controlled approximately 50-60% effectively.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: Getting Disarmament Back on Track, February 2005

2006.assessment:
Taliban insurgency intensified dramatically in 2006, contesting significant parts of
Helmand, Kandahar, Zabul, and Uruzgan provinces. NATO took command of ISAF and
expanded south, facing serious fighting. Taliban controlled or contested approximately
30-35% of districts. Government had nominal authority over much of the south but
effective control was contested.

sources.citation: ICG, Countering Afghanistan's Insurgency, October 2006

2007.assessment:
Taliban expanded territorial reach in 2007, with operations spreading from the south
to the east and threatening stability in western and northern regions. Reports indicated
Taliban were establishing shadow governance in contested areas. Government control was
described as increasingly fragile in much of rural Afghanistan. 'Country in serious
danger of falling into Taliban control' per analysts.

sources.citation: ICG, Afghanistan: The Need for International Resolve, February 2008

2008.assessment:
Taliban territorial influence continued expanding in 2008. Assessments indicated Taliban
maintained permanent presence in approximately half of the country's districts. Government
controlled the main cities and major highways but insurgents moved freely in much of rural
Afghanistan. The Kandahar-Kabul road was frequently attacked.

sources.citation: Brookings Afghanistan Index, December 2008; ICG reports

2009.assessment:
By 2009, Taliban had shadow governance structures in much of the south and east. ISAF
commander General McChrystal's assessment described the situation as 'serious.' The US
surge was announced in December. Government controlled major cities and district centers
but insurgents effectively contested or controlled much of rural Afghanistan.

sources.citation: McChrystal Assessment, August 2009; Brookings Afghanistan Index, June 2009

2010.assessment:
The US surge pushed Taliban back from some areas in 2010, particularly in Marjah and
parts of Helmand. However, Taliban maintained control or influence over large portions
of the south and east. District-level assessments showed government controlling or
influencing approximately 50-55% of districts, Taliban 10-15%, contested ~35%.

sources.citation: Brookings Afghanistan Index, December 2010; ISAF reporting

2011.assessment:
Surge reached its peak in 2011 with 100,000+ US troops in country. ISAF claimed gains
in southern provinces. However, Taliban retained significant presence across much of the
south and east. The transition to Afghan security forces began. Government controlled
most population centers but rural Afghanistan remained deeply contested.

sources.citation: Brookings Afghanistan Index, September 2012; ISAF progress reports 2011

2012.assessment:
Drawdown began in 2012 as Afghan forces took increasing responsibility. Taliban adapted
to the surge gains by shifting tactics and exploiting transition. Government maintained
major centers but Taliban influence in rural areas persisted and began recovering in
some areas where surge forces had pushed them back.

sources.citation: Brookings Afghanistan Index, September 2012
SIGAR Quarterly Reports; ICG; Brookings

2014.assessment:
As NATO combat operations ended in December 2014, Taliban began major territorial
expansion. After the transition, Taliban controlled or contested approximately 30-40%
of districts, an increase from 2012-2013. Government retained major cities but rural
areas were increasingly insecure.

sources.citation: SIGAR Quarterly Reports 2014; ICG

2015.assessment:
Taliban captured Kunduz city in September 2015 — the first provincial capital seized
since 2001. The seizure highlighted a major deterioration in government control. SIGAR
reported Afghan forces controlled or influenced approximately 65% of districts, Taliban
the remainder. Resolute Support mission (non-combat) continued.

sources.citation: SIGAR, Quarterly Report to Congress, October 2015

2016.assessment:
Taliban briefly captured Kunduz again in October 2016 and also Lashkar Gah and
Farah temporarily. SIGAR assessed government controlled/influenced approximately
57% of districts, Taliban 33%, contested 10%. A significant deterioration from 2015.

sources.citation: SIGAR, Quarterly Report to Congress, October 2016

2017.assessment:
Taliban controlled or contested approximately 40-45% of districts in 2017. SIGAR
reported government controlling/influencing approximately 56% of districts. Multiple
provincial capitals came under attack. IS-K expanded in eastern Afghanistan. Resolute
Support mission continued with US forces numbering approximately 14,000.

sources.citation: SIGAR, Quarterly Report to Congress, 2017

2018.assessment:
Territorial situation further deteriorated in 2018 with Taliban controlling or influencing
over 40% of districts. US Special Inspector General SIGAR stopped publishing district
control assessments, citing classification concerns, limiting public data. Taliban
controlled most rural areas and many district centers.

sources.citation: SIGAR Quarterly Reports 2018; Long War Journal district control map data

2019.assessment:
Taliban controlled or contested over half of Afghanistan's districts by 2019 per Long
War Journal assessments. US-Taliban peace talks were ongoing. Government retained major
cities and provincial capitals but rural areas were largely Taliban territory. Taliban's
effective governance in controlled areas continued to develop.

sources.citation: Long War Journal, Afghan District Control Map, 2019; SIGAR reports

2020.assessment:
Doha Agreement signed February 2020 committed US to withdrawal. Taliban controlled
or contested over 50% of districts. Intra-Afghan negotiations began but stalled. After
the Doha Agreement, Taliban intensified attacks on Afghan government forces while
reducing direct attacks on US/NATO forces.

sources.citation: Long War Journal, Afghan District Control 2020; SIGAR reports

2021.assessment:
In 2021, the Taliban rapidly expanded from controlling ~50% to controlling ~100% of
Afghanistan. After the final US withdrawal in late August, Taliban swept from rural areas
to provincial capitals to Kabul in weeks. The Ghani government collapsed on August 15.
By September, Taliban controlled all of Afghanistan — a complete reversal from the
Ghani government's control of major cities.

sources.citation: ACLED data 2021; multiple news sources; Long War Journal district maps

2022.assessment:
Taliban maintained control over virtually all of Afghanistan in 2022. IS-K contested
the Taliban in parts of eastern Afghanistan (Nangarhar, Kunar) but did not control
territory. Taliban conducted effective counterterrorism operations against IS-K. No
viable government opposition force existed within Afghanistan.

sources.citation: ACLED data, Afghanistan 2022; UN reports

2023.assessment:
Taliban maintained full territorial control in 2023. IS-K continued attacks but without
territorial control. Border tensions with Pakistan increased. The NRF (National Resistance
Front) conducted some operations in Panjshir but had minimal impact on territorial control.

sources.citation: ACLED data 2023; UN Security Council reporting on Afghanistan

2024.assessment:
Taliban retained full territorial control in 2024. Cross-border conflict with Pakistan
escalated, with Afghanistan launching attacks against Pakistan in early 2026. IS-K
continued attacks. Taliban governance remained unchallenged domestically.

sources.citation: Al Jazeera, Afghanistan launches attacks against Pakistan, February 2026

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet available. Taliban controls ~99% of Afghan territory; no meaningful opposition territorial control. Resistance cells in Panjshir largely suppressed.

1996.assessment:
Taliban assumed power in Kabul 1996. No budget transparency whatsoever. No functioning
Ministry of Finance, no published budget, no legislative oversight, no public financial
management system. OBI not applicable for Taliban era. Estimated score: 0/100.

sources.citation: IBP — no coverage for Taliban Afghanistan

2001.assessment:
Taliban collapsed December 2001; Bonn Agreement established Afghan Interim Authority.
No budget transparency system existed. Transition from Taliban (0/100) to nascent
reconstruction government. Pre-OBS period. Estimated scant to zero transparency.

sources.citation: IBP — OBS not started until 2006; Bonn Agreement December 2001

2002.assessment:
Afghan Interim Authority established; Loya Jirga June 2002 selected Transitional Authority.
Ministry of Finance reconstituting with donor support. No formal budget transparency system.
World Bank and donor technical assistance beginning. Estimated scant transparency (0-5/100).
OBS not yet covering Afghanistan.

sources.citation: IBP — OBS not yet covering Afghanistan 2002

2003.assessment:
Transitional Administration continuing. New Afghan constitution drafted (December 2003).
Ministry of Finance developing with World Bank support. National Budget beginning to be
published. Some budget documents produced but limited public access. Estimated scant
transparency (approximately 5/100). OBS not yet covering Afghanistan.

sources.citation: IBP — OBS not yet covering Afghanistan 2003; Afghan constitution 2003

2004.assessment:
Presidential elections October 2004; Karzai won. Constitution adopted January 2004.
Ministry of Finance developing budget systems with World Bank/ARTF support. Some budget
documents published. Very limited public access. Estimated scant transparency (5-8/100).
OBS 2006 would be first survey to include Afghanistan (scoring 8/100 in 2008).

sources.citation: IBP — OBS first edition 2006; Afghanistan budget development 2004

2005.assessment:
Parliamentary elections September 2005; Wolesi Jirga established. Budget processes
developing with donor support. Some documents published but limited public participation
in budget process. OBS 2006 (first edition) did not include Afghanistan. OBS 2008
scored Afghanistan 8/100 — this level likely prevailed in 2005-2007 period.
Estimated scant transparency (~5-8/100).

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 (first Afghanistan coverage); parliamentary elections 2005

2006.assessment:
Afghanistan Compact signed January 2006 (London Conference). Budget transparency
systems at early stage. Ministry of Finance developing with ARTF support. Based on
OBS 2008 score of 8/100, transparency was at scant level in 2006-2007 period.
Estimated 8/100 (extrapolated from 2008 OBS score).

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 (8/100) extrapolated for 2006; Afghanistan Compact January 2006

2007.assessment:
Pre-OBS 2008 year. Budget transparency estimated at same scant level as 2008 OBS score
of 8/100. Ministry of Finance published some documents but limited public access.
ARTF transparency requirements beginning to have modest impact. Extrapolated 8/100.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 (8/100) extrapolated for 2007

2008.assessment:
Afghanistan scored 8 out of 100 on the Open Budget Index 2008 — scant budget
transparency. Budget documents were largely inaccessible to the public. The
Ministry of Finance was in early stages of developing budget transparency
systems. Significant donor pressure for fiscal transparency but capacity was
severely limited. Afghanistan among the lowest scoring countries in the OBI.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2009.assessment:
Budget transparency remained at scant level in 2009, consistent with OBI 2008
score of 8/100. OBS not conducted in 2009. No significant reforms to budget
transparency framework known in this year. Interpolated from 2008 OBI score.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Afghanistan (2009 interpolation)

2010.assessment:
Afghanistan improved to 21 out of 100 in the 2010 Open Budget Survey — minimal
transparency, but a significant jump from 8 in 2008. This reflected donor-driven
improvements to public financial management systems, publishing of some budget
documents, and IMF/World Bank technical assistance. Afghanistan moved from scant
to minimal transparency threshold.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2011.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at minimal level based on 2010 OBI of 21/100.
OBS not conducted in 2011. Some continued improvements in public financial
management. Interpolated between 2010 (21) and 2012 (59) — 2011 likely
improvement phase.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2010 and 2012, Afghanistan (interpolated for 2011)

2012.assessment:
Afghanistan achieved 59 out of 100 on the 2012 Open Budget Survey — a remarkable
jump from 21 in 2010. This placed Afghanistan in the limited/sufficient range
and above many peer countries. Strong donor (US, World Bank, IMF) investment in
public financial management reform, particularly through the Afghanistan
Reconstruction Trust Fund (ARTF) and budget transparency requirements.
The Ministry of Finance published citizen budgets, pre-budget statements, and
in-year budget reports. This was Afghanistan's highest OBI score and reflected
the ISAF-era PFM investment.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2013.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated near 2012 OBI level of 59/100. OBS not conducted
in 2013. No significant regression expected in this year. PFM systems continued
functioning. Interpolated between 2012 (59) and 2015 (42).

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2012 and 2015, Afghanistan (interpolated for 2013)

2014.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated in declining trajectory as ISAF drawdown reduced
donor pressure. Interpolated between 2012 (59) and 2015 (42). NUG formation
and political crisis may have disrupted PFM consistency. Estimated limited
transparency.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2012 and 2015, Afghanistan (interpolated for 2014)

2015.assessment:
Afghanistan scored 42 out of 100 in the 2015 Open Budget Survey — limited
transparency, declined from 59 in 2012. The decline reflected post-ISAF
transition pressures, reduced donor conditionality, and NUG political disruptions
affecting PFM consistency. Still above many developing country peers but
a significant regression from the 2012 peak.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2016.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at 2015 OBI level of 42/100. OBS not conducted
in 2016. No significant changes to PFM framework known. Interpolated between
2015 (42) and 2017 (49).

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2015 and 2017, Afghanistan (interpolated for 2016)

2017.assessment:
Afghanistan scored 49 out of 100 in the 2017 Open Budget Survey — limited
transparency, slight improvement from 42 in 2015. Some recovery in PFM
systems under NUG reform commitments. Afghanistan Reconstruction Trust Fund
transparency requirements continued to drive improvements. Limited category.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2018.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at 2017 OBI level of 49/100. OBS not conducted

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2017 and 2019, Afghanistan (interpolated for 2018)

2019.assessment:
Afghanistan scored 50 out of 100 in the 2019 Open Budget Survey — limited
transparency, stable relative to 2017. PFM systems maintained despite ongoing
conflict. Budget documents published by Ministry of Finance. ARTF continued

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2020.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at 2019 OBI level of 50/100. OBS not conducted
in 2020. COVID-19 affected some budget processes. Doha Agreement and political
uncertainty. Limited transparency maintained. Interpolated between 2019 (50)
and 2021 (43).

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2019 and 2021, Afghanistan (interpolated for 2020)

2021.assessment:
Afghanistan scored 43 out of 100 in the 2021 Open Budget Survey — the survey
likely captured pre-Taliban-takeover period (August 2021). Modest decline from
50 in 2019. After Taliban takeover in August 2021, all PFM systems collapsed
and budget transparency became effectively zero. The OBI score represents
the republic period; post-August actual transparency was zero.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2022.assessment: Afghanistan excluded from IBP Open Budget Survey 2023. Taliban administration publishes no budget documents. Score would be effectively 0 but IBP did not formally survey the country post-Taliban return.

2023.assessment:
Afghanistan scored 0 out of 100 on the 2023 Open Budget Survey — complete
opacity under Taliban rule. No budget documents published. No executive budget
proposal accessible. No mid-year or year-end reports. Taliban governance
characterized by complete budget opacity. Afghanistan at the absolute bottom
of global budget transparency rankings.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2023, Open Budget Index score for Afghanistan

2024.assessment: OBS is biennial. The 2023 survey covered 2022 data; next would be 2025 survey. No 2024-specific data available. Afghanistan remains excluded from OBS under Taliban rule.

2025.assessment: IBP Open Budget Survey 2025 edition not yet published. Afghanistan likely excluded from survey under Taliban rule.

2021.assessment: Corruption score of -1.15 in 2021 reflects systemic governance failure. The Afghan government became increasingly dependent on donor aid while corruption at all levels—from police checkpoints to ministerial procurement—remained pervasive.

sources.citation: World Bank WGI, CC family, Afghanistan, 2021

2022.assessment: Corruption score was -1.18 in 2022 under Taliban rule. Formal corruption metrics changed character: Taliban imposed harsh informal enforcement in some areas but operated an opaque patronage economy. Score reflects continued institutional weakness.

sources.citation: World Bank WGI, CC family, Afghanistan, 2022

2023.assessment: Corruption score was -1.15 in 2023 under Taliban rule. Formal corruption metrics changed character: Taliban imposed harsh informal enforcement in some areas but operated an opaque patronage economy. Score reflects continued institutional weakness.

sources.citation: World Bank WGI, CC family, Afghanistan, 2023

2024.assessment: No WGI Control of Corruption data for 2024. Pre-1996 data not collected by WGI.

sources.citation: World Bank WGI, CC family, Afghanistan, 2024

2025.assessment: No WGI Control of Corruption data for 2025. Pre-1996 data not collected by WGI.

sources.citation: World Bank WGI, CC family, Afghanistan, 2025

2026.assessment: No WGI Control of Corruption data for 2026. Pre-1996 data not collected by WGI.

sources.citation: World Bank WGI, CC family, Afghanistan, 2026

1996.assessment:
Taliban rule. No EITI membership, no extractive transparency framework. Afghanistan's
mining sector was minimal and informal under Taliban. Lapis lazuli and emerald
mining in Northern Alliance-controlled areas, and some coal mining elsewhere, operated
without any disclosure requirements. No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan country page.' eiti.org.
EITI. 'Afghanistan country page.' eiti.org.

2002.assessment:
Post-Taliban reconstruction. No EITI membership — EITI itself was only proposed
by UK PM Tony Blair at WSSD in 2002 and formally launched in 2003. Afghanistan's
mining sector being assessed by international donors. No disclosure framework.
No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'About EITI - History.' eiti.org.

2003.assessment:
EITI formally launched 2003. Afghanistan not yet a member. Mining sector
interest growing with international community but no disclosure framework.
No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan country page.' eiti.org.

2004.assessment:
Afghanistan not an EITI member. Mining Law 2005 in preparation. International
investors beginning to assess large mineral deposits (Aynak copper, Hajigak
iron ore). No extractive transparency framework. No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: World Bank. 'Afghanistan Mining Sector Overview.' 2004.

2005.assessment:
Afghanistan not an EITI member. Mining Law enacted 2005. Large mining concessions
beginning. No extractive revenue disclosure. No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan country page.' eiti.org.

2006.assessment:
Not EITI member. MCC (China Metallurgical Group) bid for Aynak copper mine
in process. Significant mining sector development occurring without transparency
framework. No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: Global Witness. 'Afghanistan Mining Revenue.' 2006.

2007.assessment:
Not EITI member. MCC awarded Aynak copper mine contract in 2007 ($3.5 billion).
Growing mining sector without disclosure framework. International pressure for
extractive transparency increasing. No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: Global Witness. 'Afghanistan's Mineral Wealth.' 2007.

2008.assessment:
Not EITI member. USGS survey revealed Afghanistan had $1 trillion+ in mineral
deposits (published 2010). Mining sector growing without disclosure framework.
Donor pressure for EITI membership beginning. No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan country page.' eiti.org.

2009.assessment:
Afghanistan formally applied to join EITI in 2009. Candidacy process initiated.
Not yet admitted as candidate. Mining sector growing. No membership yet, minimal
disclosure. Application pending.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan application.' 2009. eiti.org.

2010.assessment:
Afghanistan admitted as EITI Candidate member in February 2010. This marked a
significant step in extractive transparency — candidate status requires establishing
multi-stakeholder group and beginning revenue disclosure process. First EITI report
being prepared. EITI candidate status with disclosure framework beginning.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan admitted as Candidate.' February 2010. eiti.org.

2011.assessment:
EITI candidate status continued. First EITI report covering 2009-2010 produced.
Multi-stakeholder group (MSG) active. Significant improvement in extractive
revenue disclosure framework. EITI candidate or suspended category — candidate
phase actively progressing.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI Report 2009-2010.' eiti.org.

2012.assessment:
EITI candidate status. Regular EITI reports published. Multi-stakeholder group
functioning. Extractive revenue data (mining and hydrocarbons) disclosed annually.
Candidate phase with growing extractive transparency framework.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI Report 2012.' eiti.org.

2013.assessment:
EITI candidate status. Regular annual reports. MSG active. Preparation for
compliance validation. Growing extractive transparency with EITI framework.
Candidate phase.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI Report 2013.' eiti.org.

2014.assessment:
EITI candidate/validation process. EITI validation conducted. Afghanistan
met some but not all compliance criteria. NUG formation and political transition
challenged MSG functioning. Candidate status maintained.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan Validation Report.' 2014. eiti.org.

2015.assessment:
EITI candidate/suspended. Afghanistan had compliance challenges — NUG
formation disrupted MSG functioning. EITI secretariat expressed concerns.
Candidate status with compliance challenges. Growing extractive transparency
despite political disruption.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI Status 2015.' eiti.org.

2016.assessment:
EITI candidate status. Regular EITI reports published covering mining, oil and
gas revenue. MSG active. Extractive transparency framework functioning despite
NUG political challenges. Candidate status maintained.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI Report 2015-2016.' eiti.org.

2017.assessment:
EITI candidate status. EITI reports published. MSG functioning. Challenges
with MSE participation and disclosure coverage. Candidate status maintained.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI Report 2016.' eiti.org.

2018.assessment:
EITI candidate status through most of 2018. Compliance challenges mounting.
EITI Board expressed concerns about MSG (multi-stakeholder group) civil society
participation. Afghanistan approaching suspension. Candidate status.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan status update 2018.' eiti.org.

2019.assessment:
EITI suspended Afghanistan in January 2019 due to failure to meet EITI Standard
requirements — specifically civil society participation in the MSG was inadequate.
Suspension meant loss of active EITI status. Candidate or suspended category.
Suspension triggered corrective action requirements.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Board Decision: Afghanistan suspended.' January 2019. eiti.org.

2020.assessment:
EITI suspension lifted in 2020 after Afghanistan addressed corrective action
requirements. Afghanistan reinstated as EITI member. Reports published.
EITI compliant with corrective actions category — reinstated but with ongoing
compliance conditions.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan suspension lifted 2020.' eiti.org.

2021.assessment:
EITI reinstated. Afghanistan publishing EITI reports in first half of 2021
under republic government. Taliban takeover August 2021 halted EITI operations.
MSG dissolved. EITI secretariat assessed implications of Taliban takeover.
Republic-period: compliant with corrective actions. Taliban-period: effectively suspended.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI and Taliban Takeover Assessment.' 2021. eiti.org.

2022.assessment:
EITI maintained technical member status but Afghanistan was under corrective
actions review due to Taliban takeover disruption of MSG. EITI Board assessing
whether Taliban government could fulfill EITI requirements. Corrective actions
period.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan status 2022.' eiti.org.

2023.assessment:
EITI maintaining Afghanistan membership under review due to Taliban governance.
Taliban unable to fulfill EITI multi-stakeholder requirements (civil society
participation, open government). Corrective actions period extended. Candidate
or suspended/corrective status.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan EITI status under Taliban.' 2023. eiti.org.

2024.assessment:
EITI delisted Afghanistan in June 2024 — complete removal from EITI membership.
Reason: Taliban government's inability to fulfill EITI Standard requirements,
specifically civil society and independent media participation in the MSG, and
broader governance requirements incompatible with Taliban rule. No membership,
minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan delisted.' June 2024. eiti.org.

2025.assessment:
Post-delisting. No EITI membership. No extractive transparency framework.
Taliban mining sector operations without disclosure. Afghanistan outside EITI.
No membership, minimal disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI. 'Afghanistan country page.' eiti.org.

1996.assessment:
Taliban Islamic Emirate operated on decree-based rule by Supreme Leader Mullah
Omar and Islamic Shura councils. No accessible legal framework — Taliban governance
rejected codified law in favor of Taliban interpretation of Sharia. No constitution,
no published legislation, no accessible court system for general population.
Women barred from courts. Taliban judicial decisions arbitrary and non-public.
Complete absence of accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: Rashid, Ahmed. 'Taliban.' Yale University Press, 2000.

1997.assessment:
Taliban decree rule continued. No accessible legal framework. Laws and regulations
issued verbally by Taliban commanders or Ministry for Propagation of Virtue and
Prevention of Vice. No published legal texts accessible to public. No accessible
framework.

sources.citation: Rashid, Ahmed. 'Taliban.' Yale University Press, 2000.

1998.assessment:
Taliban decree rule. No accessible legal framework. Arbitrary enforcement of
Taliban interpretation of Islamic law. Public executions and amputations conducted
without judicial process accessible to observers. No published legislation.
Complete absence of accessible legal transparency.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch. 'Taliban Abuses.' 1998-1999.

1999.assessment:
Taliban decree rule continued. No codified, accessible legal system. PVPV
(Vice and Virtue Police) enforced Taliban decrees arbitrarily. No accessible
framework for legal transparency.

sources.citation: Amnesty International. 'Afghanistan: Continuing Atrocities.' 1999.

2000.assessment:
Taliban decree rule. No accessible legal framework. Arbitrary enforcement.
UNSC 1333 documented Taliban governance failures but no change to Taliban
legal opacity. Complete absence of accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: UN Security Council. 'S/2000/1106 Afghanistan Report.' 2000.

2001.assessment:
Taliban decree rule through August 2001. Post-Bonn (December 2001), interim
authority established basic governance but with minimal legal framework — mainly
decree rule by Karzai. Constitution being drafted. Near-zero legal transparency
for both pre- and post-Taliban periods. Transition year.

sources.citation: Bonn Agreement. 'Agreement on Provisional Arrangements.' December 5, 2001.

2002.assessment:
Transitional Authority operating under minimal rule-by-decree. Constitutional
Loya Jirga preparing. Legal framework rebuilding from zero. Karzai interim
authority issued decrees. Some pre-Taliban legal traditions being restored.
Very minimal legal transparency — decree-based with some published orders.
WJP index not yet available for Afghanistan.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Judicial Reform in Afghanistan.' Asia Report No. 45, 2003.

2003.assessment:
Constitutional Loya Jirga December 2003 produced new constitution. Legal framework
being built from near-zero. WJP data not yet available. Decree-based governance
prevailed but with growing legal institutional development. Courts being
re-established. Minimal rule by decree with nascent constitutional framework.

sources.citation: Rubin, Barnett. 'Crafting a Constitution for Afghanistan.' Journal of Democracy 15(3), 2004.

2004.assessment:
Constitution enacted January 2004. Presidential election October 2004. Legal
framework formally established but implementation weak — informal dispute
resolution (jirgas) dominant over formal courts in most of country. Laws
often not published or enforced consistently. WJP data not yet available.
Difficult arbitrary enforcement despite formal constitutional framework.

sources.citation: UNDP. 'Rule of Law and Access to Justice in Afghanistan.' 2004.

2005.assessment:
Parliamentary elections September 2005 established Wolesi Jirga. Legal framework
formally in place but severely weak in practice. Courts underfunded, corrupt,
and geographically inaccessible. Most Afghans relied on informal dispute
resolution. WJP index not yet available. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Afghanistan's New Legislature: Making Democracy Work.' Asia Report No. 116, 2006.

2006.assessment:
Legal framework formal but weak. Constitution in force. Courts operating but
corrupt, understaffed, and inaccessible in most provinces. Taliban challenging
formal courts in south. WJP not yet available. Difficult arbitrary enforcement
with formal-informal legal system competition.

sources.citation: USIP. 'Rule of Law in Afghanistan.' 2006.

2007.assessment:
Formal legal framework in place. Courts operated in major cities with significant
corruption. WJP Rule of Law Index started 2010 — qualitative assessment needed.
Taliban operating parallel court system in south. Difficult arbitrary enforcement
characterized by pervasive corruption, judicial inaccessibility, and Taliban
alternative justice.

sources.citation: USAID. 'Access to Justice Assessment Afghanistan.' 2007.

2008.assessment:
Legal framework formal but weak implementation. WJP index not yet available
(starts 2010). Qualitative assessment: approximately 27-32/100 range equivalent.
Courts operating in Kabul and major cities with significant corruption. Taliban
parallel courts in south offering faster (if harsher) justice. Difficult
arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on the Independence of Judges and Lawyers. 'Afghanistan Mission Report.' 2008.

2009.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. Court corruption widespread. Judicial access
limited outside major urban centers. Election dispute mechanisms overwhelmed
during the 2009 electoral crisis. Taliban parallel justice systems operating
in large areas. WJP not yet available. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: Transparency International Afghanistan. 'National Integrity System.' 2009.

2010.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index began covering Afghanistan in limited form around 2010-2012.
Open government component approximately 27-32 (×100 = 0.27-0.32). Formal legal
framework exists but severely limited in accessibility and predictability. Court
corruption, Taliban parallel justice, and geographic inaccessibility persist.
Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index. Afghanistan (approximate 2010-2012 period).

2011.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. Continued assessment in difficult arbitrary
enforcement range approximately 27-32/100. ISAF transition created uncertainty.
Judicial reform efforts stalled. Taliban parallel courts expanding geographically.

sources.citation: WJP/qualitative estimate. Afghanistan 2011.

2012.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP Rule of Law Index coverage for Afghanistan
improving. Open government indicator approximately 27-32/100. Formal legal
institutions operated but with severe constraints. Difficult arbitrary
enforcement characterized the system.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2012, Afghanistan.

2013.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP coverage for Afghanistan in approximately
27-32/100 range. Judicial reform ongoing but limited impact. Pre-election year
legal uncertainty. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2013, Afghanistan.

2014.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP Rule of Law Index for Afghanistan approximately
27-32/100. NUG formation and contested election undermined legal legitimacy.
Courts functioned in major cities with significant corruption. Difficult
arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014, Afghanistan.

2015.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. NUG dysfunction affecting legal institutions.
WJP Open Government component for Afghanistan approximately 0.27-0.32 (×100
= 27-32). Formal legal framework in place but accessibility, predictability,
and anti-corruption very low. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015, Afghanistan.

2016.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP data approximately 27-32/100 range. Formal
legal institutions in major cities. Taliban courts in much of rural south and
east. Corruption pervasive in formal judiciary. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016, Afghanistan.

2017.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP data approximately 27-32/100. Formal legal
institutions constrained. Taliban expanding control reduced formal court
coverage. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017, Afghanistan.

2018.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP data approximately 27-32/100. Formal legal
framework weakening as Taliban control expanded. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2018, Afghanistan.

2019.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP data approximately 27-32/100. Doha
Agreement period with Taliban negotiations but formal legal framework
continued in government-controlled areas. Difficult arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Afghanistan.

2020.assessment:
Legal transparency difficult. WJP data approximately 27-32/100. NUG dual
inauguration crisis undermined legal institutions. COVID-19 disrupted court
operations. Doha Agreement. Difficult arbitrary enforcement with formal
institutions under stress.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Afghanistan.

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 2021 ended republic-era formal legal framework.
Taliban abolished constitution, courts, and legal system. Taliban decree
rule replaced formal law. Taliban religious courts operated by decree with
no published framework, no appeal mechanisms, and no public access.
Complete reversion to no accessible framework.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Taliban Justice.' Asia Report No. 312, 2021.

2022.assessment:
Taliban decree rule consolidated. No published legal framework. Taliban religious
courts operate without procedural transparency or public access. Women excluded
from legal system as practitioners and largely as petitioners. No accessible
framework under Taliban governance.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Afghanistan. 'Legal and Judicial System.' 2022.

2023.assessment:
Taliban governance continued with no accessible legal framework. Taliban Supreme
Court operated under Sharia as interpreted by Supreme Leader. No published legal
code. No procedural transparency. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Afghanistan. 'Situation of Human Rights.' A/HRC/52/84, 2023.

2024.assessment:
Taliban codified gender apartheid laws (Shura decree banning women's voices,
movement, appearance in public) without any legislative process or published
legal framework. Taliban governance entirely decree-based with no accessible
framework.

sources.citation: UN Special Rapporteur on Afghanistan. 'Report A/HRC/55/80.' 2024.

2025.assessment:
Taliban decree rule continued. No accessible legal framework. Gender apartheid
codified. Taliban Supreme Court with no transparency or public procedural
framework. Complete absence of accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: UN OCHA. 'Afghanistan Situation.' 2025.

1996.assessment: RSF World Press Freedom Index did not exist in 1996. Coverage begins with the first edition (2002). Under Taliban rule, press freedom was extremely restricted, but no systematic score is available.

1997.assessment: RSF World Press Freedom Index did not exist in 1997. Coverage begins with the first edition (2002). Under Taliban rule, press freedom was extremely restricted, but no systematic score is available.

1998.assessment: RSF World Press Freedom Index did not exist in 1998. Coverage begins with the first edition (2002). Under Taliban rule, press freedom was extremely restricted, but no systematic score is available.

1999.assessment: RSF World Press Freedom Index did not exist in 1999. Coverage begins with the first edition (2002). Under Taliban rule, press freedom was extremely restricted, but no systematic score is available.

2000.assessment: RSF World Press Freedom Index did not exist in 2000. Coverage begins with the first edition (2002). Under Taliban rule, press freedom was extremely restricted, but no systematic score is available.

2001.assessment: RSF World Press Freedom Index did not exist in 2001. Coverage begins with the first edition (2002). Under Taliban rule, press freedom was extremely restricted, but no systematic score is available.

2002.assessment:
Press freedom emerged after Taliban collapse. New independent media began
operating in Kabul — Radio Sahar, Arman FM, Tolo TV established 2004 (in
preparation). RSF score 35.5 (raw) indicates difficult conditions. The interim
authority under Karzai tolerated some press activity but security conditions,
poverty, and lack of infrastructure severely limited media. Foreign journalists
active but local press in early stages.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2002, Afghanistan

2003.assessment:
Press freedom improved slightly to RSF score 40.2 (raw, problematic range).
Media landscape growing — new radio stations emerging. Constitutional Loya
Jirga covered by press. Some journalist safety issues outside Kabul. Government
generally tolerant of media but capacity very limited. Problematic conditions
driven by security rather than government censorship primarily.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2003, Afghanistan

2004.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated to RSF score 28.3 (raw, difficult). Election year
generated some media activity around presidential elections. Growing insurgency
in south created safety risks for journalists. Taliban threats against journalists
began. Tolo TV launched. Security deterioration made reporting outside Kabul
increasingly dangerous.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2004, Afghanistan

2005.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score 39.2 (raw, problematic). Media landscape continuing
to develop. Parliamentary elections generated significant coverage. Multiple
TV channels, radio stations, and print newspapers operating. Taliban threats
against journalists growing. Security deterioration south and east created
no-go zones for press.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2005, Afghanistan

2006.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score 44.3 (raw, problematic). Growing media landscape.
Afghanistan had dozens of TV channels, hundreds of radio stations by this period.
However journalist kidnappings and killings by Taliban increasing. Rioting
following Quran-burning allegations (2006 Kabul riots) damaged several media
outlets. Problematic but growing media sector.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2006, Afghanistan

2007.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score approximately 55 (raw, problematic range). Media landscape
continued growing. Journalist casualties increasing from Taliban attacks. Afghanistan
had one of largest media sectors in region per capita with international donor support.
Government generally not major obstacle; Taliban the main threat. Problematic
conditions with security-driven constraints.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2007, Afghanistan

2008.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score approximately 58 (raw, problematic range). Significant
media sector active in Kabul. Taliban threats against journalists continued.
Some journalists arrested by government for blasphemy or other charges.
Mixed environment — government generally more tolerant but security deteriorating.
Problematic conditions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2008, Afghanistan

2009.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score approximately 60 (raw, problematic). Election coverage
generated media activity. Taliban threats and violence against journalists
continuing. Government harassment of critical media. Some journalists imprisoned.
Problematic environment driven by both government and Taliban constraints.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2009, Afghanistan

2010.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score approximately 62 (raw, problematic). Media sector one
of most vibrant in region with donor support. Taliban assassinations of journalists
continuing. Government pressure on media over corruption coverage. Problematic
conditions with active vibrant media despite threats.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2010, Afghanistan

2011.assessment:
Press freedom RSF score approximately 63 (raw, problematic). Afghan media
sector active. Tolo TV, 1TV, Ariana major broadcasters. Multiple independent
newspapers. Taliban threats major constraint. Government interference episodic.
Problematic environment overall.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2011, Afghanistan

2012.assessment:
Press freedom RSF approximately 65 (raw, problematic). Active media sector.
Drawdown of ISAF generating concerns about long-term media viability without
donor funding. Taliban threats continuing. Problematic conditions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2012, Afghanistan

2013.assessment:
Press freedom RSF approximately 66 (raw, problematic). Active media landscape.
Journalist killings continued — 11 journalists killed in 2013. Taliban and ANSF
both posed risks. Government pressure on media increasing. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2013, Afghanistan

2014.assessment:
Press freedom RSF approximately 68 (raw, problematic). Election year with
significant media coverage. ISAF ended. NUG formation generated tensions.
Journalist safety worsening with Taliban expanding. Media sector vibrant
but increasingly threatened. Problematic conditions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2014, Afghanistan

2015.assessment:
Press freedom RSF approximately 69 (raw, problematic). Growing Taliban attacks
on journalists. Kunduz fell briefly. Self-censorship increasing. Media landscape
still active but security environment worsening significantly. Government also
pressuring critical reporting. Problematic.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2015, Afghanistan

2016.assessment:
Press freedom RSF approximately 70 (raw, problematic). Security for journalists
severely degraded. Taliban and IS-K both targeting media workers. Government
advertised heavily through media, creating financial dependency. Journalist
killings high. Problematic environment bordering on difficult.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2016, Afghanistan

2017.assessment:
Press freedom RSF approximately 72 (raw, problematic). Major Taliban attack on
Tolo TV killed seven employees in January 2016 (effects continuing). Journalist
deaths high. IS-K attacks on Kabul including media targets. Government pressure
on reporting. Self-censorship widespread. Problematic conditions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017, Afghanistan

2018.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorating. RSF approximately 74 (raw, problematic). April 2018
double suicide bombing targeting journalists who had arrived to cover first blast
killed 9 journalists — one of worst single-day journalist casualties globally.
Afghanistan among most dangerous countries for journalists. Problematic bordering
on difficult.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2018, Afghanistan
CPJ. 'Nine journalists killed in Kabul double bombing.' April 2018.

2019.assessment:
RSF methodological overhaul in 2013 changed scoring scale. Approximate score
around 55 (raw) in problematic/difficult range. Afghanistan continued to be
among the world's most dangerous countries for journalists. Taliban, IS-K, and
government all posed threats. Difficult conditions for press.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2019, Afghanistan

2020.assessment:
RSF score 37.7 (raw, difficult range). Media landscape still active but severely
threatened. Doha Agreement signing raised questions about press freedom in
post-agreement environment. Taliban targeted journalists in Helmand and Kandahar.
Government secret intelligence (NDS) detained journalists. Difficult conditions
worsening toward end of republic period.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2020, Afghanistan

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 2021 catastrophically reversed press freedom. Taliban
immediately began shutting down media outlets — most Kabul TV stations stopped
broadcasting within days. Women journalists banned from most roles. Journalists
fled en masse. By end of 2021, approximately 40% of media outlets closed. RSF
score declining sharply into very serious territory. Estimated difficult-to-
very-serious transition year.

sources.citation: RSF. 'Afghanistan: Taliban shuts down media.' 2021.
CPJ. 'Afghanistan Media Landscape Under Taliban.' 2021.

2022.assessment:
Very serious press freedom conditions under Taliban. Continued media closures.
Journalists arrested, beaten, detained. Women journalists effectively banned
from working. Taliban decree banned broadcasting foreign news channels.
Remaining media operate under severe self-censorship. Very serious conditions.

sources.citation: RSF. 'Afghanistan Press Freedom Index 2022.' rsf.org.

2023.assessment:
Very serious press freedom conditions continued. Taliban controlled all state
media. Independent media severely reduced. Journalists self-censoring severely.
Women journalists unable to work. Taliban arrested journalists covering protests.
Afghanistan among worst 5 countries globally for press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF. 'Afghanistan Press Freedom 2023.' rsf.org.

2024.assessment:
RSF score 19.09 (raw) in 2024 — very serious press freedom environment.
Afghanistan ranked 178 out of 180 countries in RSF 2024 index. Taliban
progressively tightened media restrictions. Women journalists completely
banned from working. Remaining media reduced to approximately 30% of pre-
2021 levels. Journalists face arrest, torture, and killing for any critical
content. Near-complete absence of free press.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2024, Afghanistan

2025.assessment:
RSF score 17.88 (raw) in 2025 — very serious press freedom. Further deterioration
from 2024 (19.09). Taliban tightened regulations on remaining media. Independent
journalism effectively non-existent. Women journalists remain completely banned.
Afghanistan remains among worst globally for press freedom. Journalists face
detention, torture, and killing. Very serious conditions approaching no press
freedom.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2025, Afghanistan

1996.assessment:
Taliban assumed control of Kabul in 1996. No functioning national statistical system.
The Central Statistics Organization (CSO) ceased operations under Taliban rule. No data
collection, no national accounts, no price statistics. Statistical system completely
non-functional. WB SCI series does not cover Taliban era. Estimated: no statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI — no data for Taliban Afghanistan; CSO history documentation

1997.assessment: Taliban rule. No statistical system. CSO non-functional. No statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI — no data for Taliban Afghanistan

1998.assessment: Taliban rule. No statistical system. No statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI — no data for Taliban Afghanistan

1999.assessment: Taliban rule. No statistical system. No statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI — no data for Taliban Afghanistan

2000.assessment: Taliban rule. No statistical system. UNSC sanctions. No statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI — no data for Taliban Afghanistan

2001.assessment:
Taliban collapsed December 2001; Bonn Agreement established Afghan Interim Authority.
No statistical system existed under Taliban. Post-collapse, CSO reconstitution beginning
but no formal capacity. WB SCI series starts 2004 for Afghanistan. No statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI starts 2004 for Afghanistan; Bonn Agreement December 2001

2002.assessment:
Afghan Interim/Transitional Authority. CSO beginning to reconstitute with donor support.
No formal statistical capacity yet. WB SCI series begins 2004 for Afghanistan (score 24.44).
Estimated very poor to no statistical capacity. Below 2004 starting level of 24.44.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI 2004 (24.44) as baseline; 2002 estimated below this

2003.assessment:
Transitional Administration. CSO developing with donor support. Constitution drafted.
Statistical capacity near starting level of 2004 WB SCI (24.44). Estimated poor but
developing statistical capacity. WB SCI series starts 2004.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI 2004 (24.44) as baseline; 2003 estimated near or below

2004.assessment:
World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator score 24.44 in 2004 — poor statistical
capacity. Afghanistan's national statistics system was being rebuilt from near-zero
after Taliban rule. The Central Statistics Organization (CSO) was reconstituting.
Very limited data collection and dissemination capacity. No national census since
1979. Poor statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2004.

2005.assessment:
WB SCI score 31.11 — improving but still poor. Donor investment in statistical
systems beginning to yield results. USAID and World Bank supporting CSO capacity.
National Risk and Vulnerability Assessment conducted. Improving from very low base.
Poor statistical capacity but improving trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2005.

2006.assessment:
WB SCI score 32.22 — continued improvement. Statistical capacity building
ongoing with donor support. CSO developing household survey capacity.
GDP estimation improving. Still poor but gradual improvement trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2006.

2007.assessment:
WB SCI score 34.44 — approaching poor-moderate boundary. Statistical capacity
continuing to improve with donor support. National Accounts methodology improving.
Price statistics developing. Still poor but near-moderate.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2007.

2008.assessment:
WB SCI score 45.56 — crossed into moderate range. Significant improvement from
34.44 in 2007. Major donor investment in CSO capacity yielding results. National
Statistical Plan developed. Afghanistan Mortality Survey, NRVA conducted.
Statistical capacity improving substantially. Moderate statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2008.

2009.assessment:
WB SCI score 46.67 — moderate statistical capacity, slight improvement. CSO
developing methodological capacity. Election-year surveys. Statistical system
continues donor-supported improvement path. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2009.

2010.assessment:
WB SCI score 52.22 — moderate statistical capacity, continued improvement.
Surge period aid flows included significant statistical capacity investment.
Multiple household surveys conducted. GDP methodology improving. Moderate
and improving statistical system.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2010.

2011.assessment:
WB SCI score 51.11 — moderate statistical capacity, slight decline from 2010.
CSO operations continuing. Data collection capacity maintained. Statistical
capacity broadly stable at moderate level.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2011.

2012.assessment:
WB SCI score 50.00 — moderate statistical capacity, broadly stable. Statistical
system maintaining capacity built during ISAF era. Pre-drawdown period with
continued donor support. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2012.

2013.assessment:
WB SCI score 53.33 — moderate statistical capacity, slight improvement.
CSO developing capacity. National surveys improving coverage. Moderate and
improving statistical system ahead of ISAF transition.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2013.

2014.assessment:
WB SCI score 54.44 — moderate statistical capacity, slight improvement from
53.33. NUG formation. ISAF ended. Statistical capacity near peak of the
republic period. Moderate capacity maintained into the post-ISAF transition.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2014.

2015.assessment:
WB SCI score 51.11 — moderate statistical capacity, slight decline. Post-ISAF
transition reducing donor statistical support. CSO capacity declining slightly.
Statistical capacity broadly maintained at moderate level despite reduced aid.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2015.

2016.assessment:
WB SCI score 51.11 — moderate statistical capacity, stable. CSO operations
continuing. Statistical system maintained. Moderate capacity sustained.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2016.

2017.assessment:
WB SCI score 50.00 — moderate statistical capacity. Broadly stable. CSO
maintaining capacity despite conflict environment. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2017.

2018.assessment:
WB SCI score 50.00 — moderate statistical capacity. Stable. Statistical system
maintained at moderate level. CSO operations continuing.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2018.

2019.assessment:
WB SCI score 50.00 — moderate statistical capacity. Stable at 50 for third
consecutive year. Statistical capacity plateau. CSO maintained operations.
Moderate capacity sustained through Doha period.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2019.

2020.assessment:
WB SCI score 57.78 — moderate statistical capacity, notable improvement in
final year of republic period. CSO expanded data dissemination. COVID-19
generated new data collection activity. Afghanistan Statistics Year 2020
initiative. Highest SCI score in Afghanistan's republic history. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Afghanistan, 2020.

2021.assessment:
Taliban takeover August 2021 ended WB SCI series for Afghanistan. World Bank suspended
programs. CSO operations collapsed. No statistical capacity data available. Statistical
system effectively non-functional under Taliban. WB SCI series discontinued.

sources.citation: World Bank. Afghanistan Programs Suspended. August 2021.

2022.assessment:
No statistical capacity data. WB SCI series discontinued for Afghanistan. Taliban
governance provides minimal statistical output. CSO operations severely degraded.
No credible national statistics system functioning under Taliban rule.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI discontinued for Afghanistan; Taliban governance

2023.assessment:
Taliban rule continuing. WB SCI series discontinued. CSO produces minimal data under
Taliban oversight. No independent statistical capacity. No credible national statistics
system. Statistical transparency effectively absent.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI discontinued for Afghanistan; Taliban governance 2023

Taliban rule. WB SCI series discontinued. No credible national statistical system.
Statistical transparency absent under Taliban governance.

sources.citation: World Bank SCI discontinued; Taliban governance 2024

2025.assessment:
Taliban rule. WB SCI series discontinued. No credible national statistical system.
